clauses.description: 8. INFORMATION SECURITY  The Supplier shall comply with the requirements set out in the Information Security Annexure attached to this Schedule.    INFORMATION SECURITY ANNEXURE    [PROGRAMMING NOTE: THE FOLLOWING QUESTIONS ARE PREPARED TO ASSIST THE USER TO DECIDE WHICH FORM OF THE INFORMATION SECURITY ANNEXURE SHOULD BE ADOPTED:    1)  Does this transaction involve procurement of a low risk cloud based research/knowhow/information portal? Please refer to SCB's DOI for Information Security Compliance Clauses for the qualifying criteria for these types of transactions.  a.  If yes (and the qualifying criteria are met)  Adopt the ICS clause for low risk cloud based research/knowhow/information portals  b.  If No  Go to Q2  2)  Does the transaction involve procurement of a low risk cloud based collaboration tool ? Please refer to SCB's DOI for Information Security Compliance Clauses for the qualifying criteria for this type of transaction.  a.  If yes (and the qualifying criteria are met)  Adopt the ICS clause for low risk cloud based collaboration tools  b.  If No  Go to Q3  3)  Does the transaction involve procurement of low risk on premise software and related support services? Please refer to SCB's DOI for Information Security Compliance Clauses for the qualifying criteria for this type of transaction.  a.  If yes (and the qualifying criteria are met)  Adopt the ICS clause for Low Risk On Premise Software and Related Support  b.  If No  Go to Q4  4)  Is Third Party Security Assessment (TPSA) applicable?  a.  If Yes  Adopt the long form ICS clauses  b.  If No  Go to Q5  5)  Will the Supplier have system interface or connectivity to Group production systems/applications, system hosting or information processing facilities, that contain non public information?  a.  If Yes  Adopt the short form ICS clauses  b.  If No  Go to Q6  6)  Will SCB Group non public information be transferred to and/or hosted by the Supplier outside the environment (system, network and/or premises) of the SCB Group?  a.  If Yes  Adopt the short form ICS clauses  b.  If No  Go to Q7  7)  Is the Security Business Impact Assessment rating '4' or '5'? Please contact Business/Function to confirm.  a.  If Yes  Adopt the short form ICS clauses  b.  If No  Adopt the short form ICS clauses (but the system may remind the user that the ICS clauses are not mandatory, and can be removed in case it is pushed back by the supplier).]  1.  DEFINITIONS  Unless the context otherwise requires, capitalised terms used but not defined in this Annexure shall have the meaning given to them in the Contract Document.  [PROGRAMMING NOTE: ICS CLAUSE FOR (I) LOW RISK CLOUD BASED RESEARCH/KNOWHOW/INFORMATION PORTALS OR (II) LOW RISK CLOUD BASED COLLABORATION TOOL]  2.  INFORMATION SECURITY  2.1  The Supplier shall take all appropriate physical, technical and organisational security measures and observe Good Industry Practice to protect Customer Confidential Information against unauthorised or accidental, loss, destruction, damage, access, processing, erasure, transfer, use, modification, disclosure or misuse. This shall include measures to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of the Supplier systems and services, and the ability to restore the availability and access to the Customer Confidential Information in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident.  [PROGRAMMING NOTE: ICS CLAUSE FOR LOW RISK ON PREMISE SOFTWARE AND RELATED SUPPORT SERVICES]  3.  INFORMATION SECURITY  3.1  The Supplier shall take all appropriate physical, technical and organisational security measures and observe Good Industry Practice to protect Customer Confidential Information against unauthorised or accidental, loss, destruction, damage, access, processing, erasure, transfer, use, modification, disclosure or misuse. This shall include measures to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of the Supplier systems and services, and the ability to restore the availability and access to the Customer Confidential Information in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident.  3.2  The Supplier shall inform the Customer's Cyber Defence Centre (at security@sc.com (primary) or +60 (3) 95489990 (secondary), or such other communication channel that the Customer informs the Supplier from time to time) promptly, without undue delay, and in any event, not later than twenty four (24) hours, after the Supplier becomes aware of or reasonably suspects there has been an Incident. For the purposes of this paragraph, 'Incident' means any loss or destruction of, disclosure or misuse of, or damage or accidental or unauthorised access to, Customer Confidential Information.  [PROGRAMMING NOTE: SHORT FORM ICS CLAUSES]  For the purposes of this Annexure, the following capitalised terms shall have the following meanings:  Material Cyber Security Incident means: (i) a breach of security leading to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, access to, or exfiltration of data transmitted, stored or otherwise Processed by the Supplier or a Subcontractor; or (ii) a breach of critical assets and systems belonging to or in use by the Supplier or a Subcontractor, and in either of which case, may impact the availability of goods and/or services provided by the Supplier to the Customer under the Contract Document or could be used to further exploit the Customer's physical and information assets. A Material Cyber Security Incident includes a Relevant Incident.   RCA has the meaning given to it in paragraph 2.3.1 of this Annexure.  Relevant Incident means a Supplier Systems malfunction or IT security incident which has a severe and widespread impact on Supplier Systems, that causes or is reasonably likely to cause a severe and widespread impact on the Customer Group's operations or that materially impacts or is reasonably likely to materially impact the Customer Group's provision of services to its customers or clients.  2.  INFORMATION SECURITY  2.1  The Supplier shall take all appropriate physical, technical and organisational security measures and observe Good Industry Practice to protect Customer Confidential Information against unauthorised or accidental, loss, destruction, damage, access, processing, erasure, transfer, use, modification, disclosure or misuse. This shall include measures to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of the Supplier's systems and services, and the ability to restore the availability and access to the Customer Confidential Information in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical incident.   Material Cyber Security Incidents  2.2  The Supplier shall promptly inform the Customer without undue delay, and in any event, not later than twenty four (24) hours, after becoming aware of or reasonably suspecting there has been a Material Cyber Security Incident. The Supplier shall inform the Customer using the following communication channels:  2.2.1  Primary: the Customer's Cyber Defence Centre mailbox at security@sc.com; and  2.2.2  Secondary: the Customer's Cyber Defence Centre telephone number at +60 (3) 95489990,   (and such other communication channels notified in writing by the Customer to the Supplier from time to time), and shall provide the Customer with details of the Material Cyber Security Incident using the Customer's reporting template (as provided by the Customer to the Supplier from time to time), which shall include the following key information (the details in paragraphs 2.2.3, 2.2.4. 2.2.5 and 2.2.6 are mandatory), and which the Supplier shall provide to the best of its knowledge at the time and as fully and comprehensively as possible:   2.2.3  the Supplier legal entity name;  2.2.4  the date and time of the Material Cyber Security Incident;   2.2.5  background and details of the Material Cyber Security Incident;  2.2.6  the relevant Customer contract manager contact for the Contract Document;  2.2.7  details of any Customer Confidential Information impacted as a result of the Material Cyber Security Incident, including the sensitivity of that Customer Confidential Information;   2.2.8  details of any network connectivity between the Customer and the Supplier;   2.2.9  details of the IP address of Counterparty/Supplier machines affected by the Material Cyber Security Incident; and  2.2.10  details of the domains in use at /the Supplier's organisation.  2.3  In the event of a Material Cyber Security Incident, the Supplier shall assist the Customer in conducting any investigations and analysis that the Customer may require. The Supplier shall take all necessary steps consistent with Good Industry Practice, taking into account the severity of the risk, to resolve such Material Cyber Security Incident as quickly as possible and to prevent its recurrence. Without limitation to the foregoing, the Supplier shall:  2.3.1  perform a root cause and impact analysis for the Material Cyber Security Incident ('RCA');  2.3.2  subject to paragraph 2.3.3, promptly submit the results of the RCA to the Customer without undue delay, and in any event, not later than ten (10) days, after the Supplier first informed the Customer of the Material Cyber Security Incident pursuant to paragraph 2.2;  2.3.3  if, due to ongoing analysis, complete reporting of results in the RCA is not possible at the time of RCA submission pursuant to paragraph 2.3.2:  (A)  the Supplier shall ensure the submitted RCA reflects as fully and comprehensively as possible the current state of the analysis at the time of submission; and   (B)  the Supplier shall request, and agree with the Customer, additional period(s) of time for the Supplier to submit further RCA revisions/updates/versions (reflecting as fully and comprehensively as possible the current state of analysis at the time of submission), provided that the final RCA with complete reporting of results is submitted without undue delay, and in any event not later than twenty eight (28) days after the Supplier first informed the Customer of the Material Cyber Security Incident pursuant to paragraph 2.2; and  2.3.4  implement adequate corrective measures to prevent a recurrence of the Material Cyber Security Incident.  2.4  The Supplier acknowledges that the Customer may be obligated to notify Regulatory Authorities of Material Cyber Security Incidents. In such cases, notwithstanding any provision in the Contract Document to the contrary, such notification shall not constitute a breach of the obligations of confidence under the Contract Document by the Customer.  2.5  This Annexure shall survive termination or expiry of the Contract Document.  [PROGRAMMING NOTE: LONG FORM ICS CLAUSES]  For the purpose of this Annexure, the following capitalised terms shall have the following meanings:  Card means any credit or debit, or any other form of payment card.  Cardholder Data means all Card details, including the account number, cardholder name, expiry date and card verification number.  Data Breach means any loss or destruction of, or unauthorised Processing, disclosure or misuse of, or damage or accidental or unauthorised access to, Customer Confidential Information, or failure of or failure to implement the Information Security Controls.   ICS Failures has the meaning given to it in paragraph 2.5 of this Annexure.  ICS Schedule means the Appendix attached to this Annexure titled 'Minimum Information Security and Cyber Security (ICS) Requirements Schedule For Suppliers'.  Information Security Controls has the meaning given to it in paragraph 2.1 of this Annexure .   Material Cyber Security Incident means: (i) a breach of security leading to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, access to, or exfiltration of data transmitted, stored or otherwise Processed by the Supplier or a Subcontractor; or (ii) a breach of critical assets and systems belonging to or in use by the Supplier or a Subcontractor, and in either of which case, may impact the availability of goods and/or services provided by the Supplier to the Customer under the Contract Document or could be used to further exploit the Customer's physical and information assets. A Material Cyber Security Incident includes a Relevant Incident.   RCA has the meaning given to it in paragraph 2.9.1 of this Annexure.  Relevant Incident means a Supplier Systems malfunction or IT security incident which has a severe and widespread impact on Supplier Systems, that causes or is reasonably likely to cause a severe and widespread impact on the Customer Group's operations or that materially impacts or is reasonably likely to materially impact the Customer Group's provision of services to its customers or clients, and includes a Data Breach.  Remedial Action Plan has the meaning given to it in paragraph 2.5 of this Annexure.   Technology Records means any and all information security related logs for Supplier Systems used to Process Customer Group Personal Data, Customer Data or Confidential Information, including system access logs, network logs, security logs, application logs, database logs, audit and event logs.   TPSA has the meaning given to it in paragraph 2.3 of this Annexure.   2.  INFORMATION SECURITY  2.1  The Supplier shall comply with the following security measures in relation to Customer Confidential Information (including Personal Data) ('Information Security Controls') (and in the event of any conflict between any of the Information Security Controls below, the most stringent or higher level of security standard shall apply):  2.1.1  take all appropriate physical, technical and organisational security measures and observe Good Industry Practice to protect Customer Confidential Information, in particular against accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure of, or access to Customer Confidential Information transmitted, stored or otherwise Processed. This shall include measures to ensure the ongoing confidentiality, integrity, availability and resilience of the Supplier Systems and services (as applicable), and the ability to restore the availability and access to the Customer Confidential Information in a timely manner in the event of a physical or technical security incident;  2.1.2  in relation to Processing by the Supplier of Customer Confidential Information and/or access or connection by the Supplier to Customer Group Systems, implement and maintain, as a minimum, appropriate information security controls in accordance with the ICS Schedule (as may be updated by the Customer and notified to the Supplier from time to time); and  2.1.3  at all times establish and maintain a security environment which complies with the following international standards for information security:   (A)  the ISO/IEC 27001:2013   Information Security Management Systems   Requirements;  (B)  the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) Cybersecurity Framework v1.1; and  (C)  Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS) (applicable to the extent that the Supplier will be Processing Cardholder Data).  2.2  The Supplier shall upon the Customer's request, or as soon as such retention is no longer required for the performance of the Supplier's obligations under the Contract Document, delete, destroy or return the Customer Confidential Information and storage media in accordance with a process approved in writing or notified by the Customer. Upon the Customer's request, the Supplier shall certify that deletion or destruction has taken place. The Supplier shall perform such deletion or destruction in an appropriate and auditable manner that ensures that the Customer Confidential Information cannot be re created, accessed or read, including:  2.2.1  for electronic media that will be redeployed, a sanitation process that deletes data before redeployment, which destroys boot partitions, file pointers, and user data, and prevents all data from being reconstructed and read, in accordance with Good Industry Practice guidelines for example, as set out in National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) SP800 88 or US Department of Defence (DoD) 5220.22 M Directives; and  2.2.2  for disposal of documents, by cross shredding or using a secure waste disposal process designated for the Customer Confidential Information.  THIRD PARTY SECURITY ASSESSMENT (TPSA)   2.3  Upon the Customer's written request, the Supplier shall within such reasonable notice as may be stipulated by the Customer and during working hours, complete the Bank's Third Party Security Assessment (which may be conducted onsite or remotely) providing full and accurate responses, and provide any relevant information, documentation, reports and Technology Records requested by the Bank to verify the Supplier's compliance with the Information Security Controls, and any certifications (if available) as specified in paragraph 2.4.   At the Customer's election, such TPSA may be conducted onsite or remotely by the Customer or its authorised representatives, a Regulatory Authority or an independent inspection body with the required professional qualifications selected by the Customer, subject to their respective duty of confidentiality to the Customer. The TPSA shall not exceed a frequency of one per calendar year, unless as directed by a Regulatory Authority, a Material Cyber Security Incident had occurred or there is a material change to the service scope or a change in the events described in paragraph 2.11, in that case additional incremental security assessment may occur.  2.4  For the purposes of, and without limitation to paragraph 2.3, the Customer may request any one of the following detailed reports:  2.4.1  Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard (PCI DSS)   Attestation of Compliance for Onsite Assessments (AOC), Service Providers (applicable to the extent that the Supplier will be Processing Cardholder Data); or  2.4.2  Any other independent assessment report, such as   (A)  the Statement on Standards for Attestation Engagements No. 16 (SSAE 16)   Service Organisation Control 2 (SOC 2) Type II;  (B)  International Standards on Assurance Engagement No. 3402 (ISAE 3402)   Service Organisation Control 2 (SOC 2) Type II;  (C)  Attestation of compliance with ISO 27001:2013;  (D)  Cloud Security Alliance (CSA)   Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM); or  (E)  Association of Banks in Singapore (ABS)   Outsourced Service Provider Assurance Report (OSPAR).  2.5  Any failure by the Supplier to comply with the Information Security Controls ('ICS Failures') as identified from information obtained by the Customer or any TPSA or audit conducted by the Customer under paragraph 2.3 shall be reported in writing by the Customer and be resolved by the Supplier within 90 days from the date of report. The Customer in collaboration with the Supplier shall create a remedial action plan including remediation timelines adhering to the resolution timeframes set out above to address any ICS Failures ('Remedial Action Plan'). Implementation of remedial actions shall be at the Supplier's cost.  2.6  The Customer may terminate the Contract Document for cause with immediate effect, at any time by notice in writing to the Supplier, if the Supplier fails to remediate any ICS Failures within the timeline set out in paragraph 2.5.  Material Cyber Security Incidents   2.7  The Supplier acknowledges that the Customer is obligated to notify Regulatory Authorities of certain Material Cyber Security Incidents, and the Supplier shall promptly inform the Customer of a Material Cyber Security Incident without undue delay, and in any event no later than:   2.7.1  one (1) hour, if the Material Cyber Security Incident is a Relevant Incident; or  2.7.2  twenty four (24) hours for all other Material Cyber Security Incidents,  after the Supplier becomes aware of or reasonably suspects there has been a Material Cyber Security Incident.   [DRAFTING NOTE: REPLACE 2.7.2 WITH THE BELOW CLAUSE IF THE GROUP TECHNOLOGY TRANSACTION IMPLEMENTED IN INDIA WOULD BE CATEGORISED AS MATERIAL OUTSOURCING OR IF THE INDIA SPECIFIC SINGLE COUNTRY TECHNOLOGY TRANSACTION IS CATEGORISED AS MATERIAL OUTSOURCING.]  2.7.2  two (2) hours for all other Material Cyber Security Incidents,  after the Supplier becomes aware of or reasonably suspects there has been a Material Cyber Security Incident.  2.8  The Supplier shall inform the Customer of a Material Cyber Security Incident using the following communication channels:  2.8.1  Primary: the Customer's Cyber Defence Centre mailbox at security@sc.com; and  2.8.2  Secondary: the Customer's Cyber Defence Centre telephone number at +60 (3) 95489990,  (and such other communication channels notified in writing by the Customer to the Supplier from time to time), and shall provide the Customer with details of the Material Cyber Security Incident using the Customer's reporting template (as provided by the Customer to the Supplier from time to time), which shall include the following key information (the details in paragraphs 2.8.3, 2.8.4, 2.8.5, 2.8.6 and 2.8.7 are mandatory), and which the Supplier shall provide to the best of its knowledge at the time and as fully and comprehensively as possible:  2.8.3  the Supplier legal entity name;  2.8.4  the date and time of the Material Cyber Security Incident;   2.8.5  background and details of the Material Cyber Security Incident, including the volume and types of data affected, the likely consequences of the Material Cyber Security Incident, and the categories and approximate number of individuals concerned (if applicable);  2.8.6  a description of the measures taken or proposed to be taken by the Supplier to address the Material Cyber Security Incident (including where appropriate, measures to mitigate its possible adverse effects);  2.8.7  the relevant Customer contract manager contact for the Contract Document;  2.8.8  details of any Customer Confidential Information impacted as a result of the Material Cyber Security Incident, including the sensitivity of that Customer Confidential Information;   2.8.9  details of any network connectivity between the Customer and the Supplier;   2.8.10  details of the IP address of Supplier machines affected by the Material Cyber Security Incident; and  2.8.11  details of the domains in use at the Supplier's organisation.  2.9  In the event of a Material Cyber Security Incident, the Supplier shall assist the Customer in conducting any investigations and analysis that the Customer may require. The Supplier shall take all necessary steps consistent with Good Industry Practice, taking into account the severity of the risk, to resolve such Material Cyber Security Incident as quickly as possible and to prevent its recurrence. Without limitation to the foregoing, the Supplier shall:   2.9.1  perform a root cause and impact analysis for the Material Cyber Security Incident ('RCA');   2.9.2  subject to paragraph 2.9.3, promptly submit the results of the RCA to the Customer without undue delay, and in any event, not later than ten (10) days after the Supplier first informed the Customer of the Material Cyber Security Incident pursuant to paragraph 2.7;  2.9.3  if, due to ongoing analysis, complete reporting of results in the RCA is not possible at the time of RCA submission pursuant to paragraph 2.9.2:  (A)  the Supplier shall ensure the submitted RCA reflects as fully and comprehensively as possible the current state of the analysis at the time of submission; and   (B)  the Supplier shall request, and agree with the Customer, additional period(s) of time for the Supplier to submit further RCA revisions/updates/versions (reflecting as fully and comprehensively as possible the current state of analysis at the time of submission), provided that the final RCA with complete reporting of results is submitted without undue delay, and in any event, not later than twenty eight (28) days after the Supplier first informed the Customer of the Material Cyber Security Incident pursuant to paragraph 2.7; and  2.9.4  implement adequate corrective measures to prevent a recurrence of the Material Cyber Security Incident.  2.10  Without limiting paragraph 2.7, the Supplier acknowledges that the Customer may be obligated to notify Regulatory Authorities of Material Cyber Security Incidents. In such cases, notwithstanding any provision in the Contract Document to the contrary such notification shall not constitute a breach of the obligations of confidence under the Contract Document by the Customer.  Changes  2.11  The Supplier shall notify the Customer as soon as practicable from when it first becomes aware of:  2.11.1  any material changes to Supplier Systems retaining or hosting Customer Confidential Information on behalf of the Customer;  2.11.2  any change to the country location(s) or data centres where the Customer Confidential Information is retained, hosted or accessed from; or  2.11.3  any change in relation to Subcontractors engaged in Processing Customer Confidential Information in relation to the Contract Document.  2.12  The Supplier shall inform the Customer as soon as reasonably practicable (or such timescale agreed in writing between the Parties to meet the requirements of a Regulatory Authority) from when it first becomes aware of any relevant change of laws or regulations applicable to the Supplier or its Subcontractors affecting the Supplier's ability to comply with the terms of the Contract Document in relation to Processing Customer Confidential Information.   2.13  This Annexure shall survive the termination or expiry of the Contract Document.     APPENDIX  Minimum Information Security and Cyber Security (ICS) Requirements Schedule for Suppliers  (Version 6 June 2025)    General Statement  The Supplier Processing SC Group Data and/or using SC Group Systems, needs to establish and implement formal procedures and documentation (including an Information Security Policy) approved by an appropriate level of the Supplier management covering, at a minimum, the security requirements set out in this Schedule.    Such documentation and procedures must be reviewed and approved by an appropriate level of the Supplier management at least every two (2) years and must be communicated in writing to staff who Process or have access to SC Group Data or infrastructure used to Process it.     The security requirements set out in this Schedule are applicable to the Supplier's Systems and Staff Processing SC Group Data.  1.  Human resource security  1.1.  Information security awareness training must be conducted at least annually for all Supplier Staff having access to SC Group Data.  1.2.  Prior to the engagement of Supplier Staff, the Supplier must ensure appropriate background checks are performed on all Supplier Staff who has technical, administrative, or physical access to SC Group Data.    1.3.  The roles and responsibilities of Supplier Staff in relation to information security must be defined and documented.  2.  Information security risk management  2.1.  An information security risk assessment of internal and external cyber threats must be performed at least annually on all critical information assets, information systems and processes relevant to the Services rendered to SC Group, including but not limited to:   a)  identification of the information security risks associated with the above mentioned critical information assets, information systems and processes; and  b)  risk remediation activities for each identified information security risk, which include identification and implementation of appropriate mitigating controls and remediation timelines.  2.2.  An information security risk register must be maintained to monitor the progress of all remediation activities, with regular reporting to an appropriate level of management.  3.  Information asset management   3.1.  The Supplier shall maintain an up to date asset inventory for each component of Supplier Systems (including both internal and external assets such as software, applications, firmware and hardware components, including virtual resources where applicable) used to Process SC Group Data. The inventory must clearly identify the relevant Supplier Staff members responsible for managing each asset. The inventory must be reviewed annually, or upon any change to assets or responsible Supplier Staff, whichever is sooner, to ensure information is accurate, complete, and up to date.  3.2.  Unauthorised and/or (subject to section 3.3) unsupported physical and software/application assets in Supplier Systems must be identified and removed/remediated.  3.3.  Closely monitor the end of support (EOS) / end of life (EOL) dates for hardware and software assets used in Supplier Systems that Process SC Group Data and proactively manage the lifecycle of such assets to ensure they remain under support and receiving updates and security patches. Assets used beyond EOS/EOL dates must have undergone risk assessment and effective security risk management and mitigations in place prior to the EOS/EOL date.  3.4.  Retention requirements for SC Group Data Processed by the Supplier must be established and followed in accordance with the Agreement.  3.5.  Assets must be formally managed throughout removal, transfers, end of life (EOL) and secure disposal or reuse.  3.6.  The Supplier shall securely wipe all SC Group Data and configurations (such as certificates and access credentials) stored on electronic storage media/hardware (including portable storage devices) using appropriate data wiping and erasure techniques that ensure SC Group Data and configurations are rendered permanently irretrievable: (a) upon the expiry of Supplier's obligation to Process SC Group Data under the Agreement; and (b) prior to any such media/hardware being reused, returned or otherwise transferred to a third party, destroyed or sent for repair.  3.7.   Paper waste shall be securely disposed of when no longer required and management of paper waste disposal must include the following elements:  a)  shredder or locked paper waste disposal containers readily available to Supplier Staff;   b)  confidential waste bins are locked;  c)  confidential waste bins are emptied by authorised personnel on a regular basis; and  d)  keys to confidential waste bins are managed in a secure manner.  3.8.  The Supplier shall maintain an inventory of electronic storage media/hardware scheduled for destruction or disposal. Supplier shall ensure that any third party engaged in destruction or disposal of media/hardware has undergone a security risk assessment and provides the Supplier with certificates of destruction or secure disposal signed by an authorised representative.   3.9.  The Supplier shall implement and review annually a procedure for the appropriate classification of all data and assets (e.g. public to top secret).   3.10.  The Supplier shall, in respect of Supplier Systems used to Process SC Group Data, retain logs that capture security related activity and system events that are appropriate and relevant the conduct of compliance assurance, audit, regulatory, litigation, and investigation activities. Supplier shall retain all such logs in accordance with Applicable Law and at least for a reasonable time to support the activity for which it is collected.  4.  Information security incident management and notification  4.1.  Establish information security incident reporting and escalation procedures, which include appropriate timelines for investigation and escalation of information security incidents.  4.2.  Performance of root cause and impact analysis for information security incidents involving SC Group Data, and implementation of adequate corrective measures to prevent a recurrence of such incidents.  4.3.  The Supplier must:  a)  establish and implement rule based triggers that generate security alerts when known attack patterns, signatures, or behaviours are detected (e.g. number of invalid password attempts);  b)  use appropriate monitoring tools to automatically generate the above mentioned security alerts.  4.4  The Supplier shall ensure that the responsibilities and the authorities for monitoring the generated security alerts are assigned and communicated to the Supplier Staff.  5.  Data leakage prevention   5.1.  The Supplier shall use secure encryption and protocols to ensure the security, confidentiality, and integrity of all SC Group Data Processed by the Supplier. Selection and implementation of cryptographic algorithms and protocols must follow Good Industry Practice and comply with any legal and regulatory related encryption requirements under Applicable Laws.  5.2.  Supplier shall, and shall ensure that all Supplier Staff, only Process SC Group Data on portable devices that have been approved and issued by the Supplier. The Supplier shall protect all SC Group Data Processed on portable devices against loss, damage, and unauthorised access or disclosure, using appropriate security measures including, without limitation, the measures set out in this Section 5.  5.3.  The Supplier shall ensure that data leakage prevention (DLP) measures are deployed to monitor for unauthorised access and disclosure of SC Group Data across all Supplier approved communication channels such as email, internet access, portable storage devices and network channels. The Supplier shall block Supplier Staff access to all unapproved communication channels and services to prevent malicious or unintentional SC Group Data disclosure.  5.4.  Access to ports such as USB, FireWire or any similar removable media interfaces must be disabled by default (and formal access requests with business justification must be approved prior to unblocking such restriction).  5.5.  The Supplier must ensure that laptops Processing SC Group Data protect the confidentiality of stored information by:  a)  Deploying file based encryption software tools that enable users to safeguard files and folders as required;   b)  Being centrally managed unless the Supplier has granted specific approval otherwise based on a justified exemption and where appropriate security mitigations are in place to protect SC Group Data. Any such exception must be granted in accordance with the Supplier's documented procedures;   c)  Disabling any capability that permits software to be automatically run, launched, or executed without any user input (i.e. disabling auto run features).  5.6.  The Supplier shall log and monitor Supplier Staff printing for potential misuse and disclosure of SC Group Data, ensure business access limitations enforcement and restrict Supplier Staff from printing to non Supplier printers.  5.7.   If Supplier Staff has been provided with a Corporate Owned Device (COD) or are permitted to use a personal mobile device under the Bring Your Own Mobile Device (BYOMD) scheme to Process SC Group Data, the Supplier must deploy appropriate security measures to protect SC Group Data on such devices, including:  a)  deploy appropriate mobile device management (MDM) applications;   b)  containerisation of SC Group Data (i.e. SC Group Data is segregated and secured from other data on the COD or the BYOMD);  c)  implement and configure remote wipe capabilities; and  d)  enforce automatic updates and periodic scans of the devices, to check for jailbreaking/rooting/tampering and malware.   5.8.  Circumvention of, or tampering with, the Supplier COD or BYOMD security measures and the MDM is prohibited and the compromised COD and BYOMD will not be granted access to SC Group Data.  5.9.  Supplier shall ensure that its policies and protocols ensure that it retains effective and lawful control over SC Group Data Processed on such devices. Supplier shall define the circumstances in which the Supplier reserves the right to:  a)  Confiscate, audit or inspect the devices;   b)  Manage the devices and applications running on it;   c)  Enforce technical security controls such as access control, malware protection and encryption;   d)  Monitor, access, manage, recover, or delete the Supplier's and personal applications and data on the device; and  e)  Remotely delete all SC Group Data in the event of a security incident if Supplier's Staff leave the organisation, or if the device is lost or stolen or if a user has exceeded the maximum number of failed password attempts.  6.  Access control  6.1.  Users must be assigned unique user IDs with clear ownership (i.e. traceable to an individual).  6.2.  Requests for all user access (including emergency, logical, ad hoc, role based, mandatory), must be approved by an appropriate level of management.  6.3.  Access to all systems is controlled by a secure log on process (e.g., authentication of user ID and password).  6.4.  When granting access permissions and authorisations as needed by the Supplier's Staff job role, the principles of least privilege must be adopted.  6.5.  Multi factor authentication must be implemented for privileged account users including, without limitation all administrator accounts or accounts with elevated privileges to perform specific administrative tasks.  6.6.  Dormant user accounts must be identified and disabled after ninety (90) days of inactivity or such earlier time if no longer needed.  6.7.  Access rights of all users, including but not limited to privileged accounts, must be recertified semi annually.  6.8.  Privileged accounts must be:   a)  restricted to authorised Supplier Staff only;  b)  granted on a ‘need to use' basis;  c)  an addition to the user holding normal user access; and   d)  used for defined security and system administrative activities.  6.9.  Passwords for user accounts with ‘privileged' or similar attributes must be securely vaulted.  6.10.  Activities of privileged accounts must be logged and reviewed.   6.11.  The passwords used by Supplier Staff Processing SC Group Data shall comply with the following password requirements:  a)  passwords for accounts with multi factor authentication must contain a minimum of eight (8) and passwords for accounts without multifactor authentication must contain a minimum of twelve (12) characters, including at least one of each: uppercase letter, lowercase letter, number, and special character;  b)  password must not be sequential (e.g. 123456, 98765, abcd);  c)  password change on first log on;  d)  password change for accounts with multi factor authentication must be required at least every hundred and eighty (180) days and accounts without multi factor authentication shall be every ninety (90);  e)  password reuse/history prohibitions: minimum last ten (10) passwords must not be allowed to be re used;  f)  user accounts must lock after three (3) consecutive failed log in attempts;   g)  session timeout including remote sessions set to fifteen (15) minutes of inactivity forcing revalidation of credentials; and  6.12.  Passwords, PINs or any such authentication values must not be displayed, stored and/or transmitted in clear text.  6.13.  Error or help messages that could facilitate unauthorised access attempts to succeed must not be shown.  6.14.  Supplier Staff access (including physical and logical access) to SC Group Data or Supplier Systems that Process SC Group Data must be removed on or before the Supplier Staff's last working day, in the event of their resignation, termination or transfer.  6.15.  Supplier Staff must be required to return all corporate assets (e.g. laptop, mobile phone, access cards and keys) and SC Group Data (whether in electronic or physical format) in their possession or control before leaving the Supplier.   6.16.  Remote access to Supplier Systems must only be granted to authorised Supplier Staff and devices and multi factor authentication must be used. Such access must be granted only with a valid business justification. This includes remote access by privileged users for administrative or system configuration tasks.  6.17.  The Supplier's remote access connections must be encrypted using TLS 1.2 (Transport Layer Security) or higher.  6.18.  All remote access connections to Supplier Systems (including by Supplier Staff) must be obtained via the Supplier's documented, approved, and dedicated secure remote access solution.  7.  IT Infrastructure Security  7.1.  Security configuration must be reviewed against internal standards on a regular basis to monitor compliance.  7.2.  Security patches must be deployed on a regular basis and as recommended by the software provider.  7.3.  Security patches must be tested before deployment on machines, endpoints, servers, databases, and network devices and are deployed according to severity in a timely manner to counter latest security vulnerabilities exploitation.   7.4.  The Supplier shall develop, document, and maintain the following requirements in its baseline security standards for the Supplier's systems and applications used to Process SC Group Data:  a)  change or remove factory or vendor default accounts;   b)  change default or guessable account passwords to meet enterprise password requirements as described in Section 6;  c)  remove or disable unnecessary software;  d)  use only secure protocols for configurations;  e)  set hardware BIOS (basic input/basic output system) and boot options;  f)  prevent the use of host IP based authentication; and  g)  configure clock synchronisation.  8.  System development and change management  8.1.  Software development related change requests must be initiated through a formal change request process, including risk and impact analysis, and approved by an appropriate level of the Supplier's management.  8.2.  Security requirements (including but not limited to legal, regulatory and privacy protection requirements under Applicable Law or otherwise applied in accordance with Good Industry Practice) must be applied in the systems development and change management processes.  8.3.  The Supplier shall include potential threats and review industry standards in accordance with Good Industry Practice at the design phase, for example by leveraging lists such as OWASP Top 10 Most Critical Web Application Security Risks and CWE/SANS Top 25 Most Dangerous Software Errors or any other industry recognised practises.  8.4.  SC Group Data must not be used for testing unless authorised in writing by the designated SC authorised representative (and/or such other SC contact(s) notified in writing by SC to the Supplier).  8.5.  Source code review must be conducted prior to implementation of changes to the production environment of a system.  8.6.  The Supplier shall ensure that developers and testers access rights are revoked, and any test data is securely removed before deploying to production.   8.7.  Development and testing environments must be separated from production environments.   8.8.  Developers must have time limited access to production environments.  8.9.  The Supplier shall ensure that:  a)  technology assets and/or services are security assessed (including for security architecture);  b)  for all software/applications (for which the source code is not available, such as commercial off the shelf software applications) SAST (and, if not available, DAST or penetration testing) reports are conducted; and  c)  the environments used for application or software development are compliant with information security best practices and must have documented independent audits or established compliance roadmaps in alignment with industry standard certifications (for example: SSAE18 SOC 2 reports, ISO27001/2, NIST Cyber Security Framework, FIPS 140 2 etc.).  9.  Network security  9.1.  The Supplier shall install protection solutions (e.g. anti malware, intrusion detection) on the Supplier's Systems. The installed solutions must be managed centrally so that policy enforcement and configuration is carried out centrally, including an automated malware definition refresh.  9.2.  Firewalls protecting networks from malicious traffic must be implemented and maintained, including reviewing the firewall rules and exceptions on an annual basis or semi annually where PCI DSS requirements apply, with such review including removal of expired or unnecessary rules, resolution of conflicting rules and addition of new rules.  9.3.  The Supplier shall ensure that external routers and firewalls are configured in the following manner:  a)  both routers and firewalls deny all traffic unless explicitly permitted;  b)  routers filter traffic through access control lists (ACL);   c)  use anti spoofing filter; and  d)  use stealth mode.  9.4.  The Supplier must ensure that any change to the network devices, network channels, network links and network segments configuration generates an alert. The alerts must be monitored and validated to ensure that all changes are approved and follow the Supplier's change management process.  9.5.  The Supplier shall review the IDS/IPS devices/software on a weekly basis to check if signatures have been updated timely and successfully.  9.6.  The Supplier shall ensure that information systems are segregated on networks based on trust levels.  9.7.  The Supplier's wireless networks must be configured with appropriate authentication mechanisms including network access control, device authentication, user authentication (e.g. multi factor authentication), end to end encryption standard of at least WPA2 (Wi Fi Protected Access 2), TLS 1.2 or 1.3 (Transport Layer Security) or equivalent.  9.8.  Guest networks must be segregated from Supplier corporate production networks via the use of firewalls.  9.9.  The Supplier shall ensure that security architecture principles are incorporated into design and controls of the Supplier's network devices, network channels, network links and network segments (e.g. 'secure by design', 'defence in depth', 'secure by default', 'default deny', 'fail secure', 'secure in deployment' and 'usability and manageability').   10.  Physical security   10.1.  Where Supplier Facilities are used to Process SC Group Data and/or store SC Group Data backup:   a)  physical security mechanisms are implemented, maintained, monitored, and periodically recertified to restrict access to Supplier Systems and Supplier Facilities; and  b)  a process is established for granting and approving access to Supplier Facilities.  10.2.  Physical and environmental controls are implemented to protect locations where SC Group Data is Processed, including (but not limited to):  a)  access control;  b)  smoke and fire suppression;  c)  power and voltage monitoring;  d)  temperature control;  e)  humidity control; and  f)  water leak monitoring.  10.3.  The Supplier shall handle non electronic data (i.e. physical records) with utmost care and security by, including but not limited to:  a)  keeping the data securely locked away when not actively in use;  b)  not accessing the data in public places that could lead to unauthorised disclosure;  c)  not disposing the data outside of Supplier Facilities without the written consent from SC Group, and  d)  not discussing or otherwise exposing the contents of any SC Group Data to voice activated devices or services (for example digital assistants like Amazon Alexa or Google Assistance).   11.  Information security aspects of backup   11.1.  The Supplier must ensure that access protection measures are implemented to protect all backup infrastructure Processing SC Group Data against unauthorised tampering or changes, including but not limited to destructive ransomware attacks.  11.2.  The Supplier must ensure the key and the encryption algorithm for backup encryption are managed in line with the Agreement and Section 12 (Cryptographic Management).  12.  Cryptographic management   12.1.  The Supplier must ensure the usage of the Supplier's approved protocols and cryptographic algorithms when Processing SC Group Data and that cryptographic algorithms complies with Good Industry Practice and all encryption related legal and regulatory requirements under Applicable Law.  12.2.  The Supplier must establish documented processes for managing cryptographic keys (including software based and hardware security modules), in a manner which is witnessed and recorded (such as via key ceremonies) and which covers:  a)  key life cycle management (generation using approved algorithms and key lengths, rotation, renewal, revocation, destruction);  b)  secure distribution, activation, and storage of keys;  c)  immediate revocation (deactivation) of cryptographic keys (e.g. if a key is compromised, or a key owner changes job or leaves the organisation);  d)  key validity period;   e)  restriction of access to cryptographic keys to authorised Supplier Staff;  f)  sharing of cryptographic keys (e.g. using split key generation) required for protecting confidential information including SC Group Data and Supplier Systems; and  g)  restricting re use of compromised, revoked, or expired cryptographic keys for encryption of any new data.  12.3.  The Supplier shall ensure that cryptographic keys are stored, at the minimum, in FIPS 140 2 level 2 validated key vaults.  12.4.  In the event that the Supplier uses a cloud service provider to Process SC Group Data, the Supplier may use the native encryption provided by the cloud service provider where such encryption meets the requirements set out under this Schedule.  12.5.  The Supplier shall protect SC Group Data at rest, at the minimum, by ensuring:  a)  application level encryption;  b)  column level encryption on all databases that Process SC Group Data, e.g. symmetric encryption for SQL Server and Oracle databases;  c)  file level or folder level encryption on all devices and storage media that Process SC Group Data, using 128 bit Advanced Encryption Standard (AES 128) at the minimum; and   d)  full disk encryption on all devices (mobile devices and portable storage devices e.g. laptops, external hard drives) that Process SC Group Data, using AES 128 at the minimum.  12.6.  The Supplier must apply encryption to protect SC Group Data during transit (transmitted via email, attachments, uploading information to websites, media, etc.) using Transport Layer Security (TLS) 1.2 at the minimum.  13.  Continuous Monitoring  13.1.  The Supplier shall ensure that all security event logs of SC Group Data processed in a centralised log management solution are protected against unauthorised access, deletion, modification and deliberate or accidental tampering.   13.2.  The Supplier shall ensure that the security event logs and reports are examined in a timely manner appropriate to the criticality of the events and alerts received from the monitoring solution.  13.3.  Supplier shall implement active anti virus and anti malware protection and ensure that anti virus and, anti malware software is configured to perform automatic software and definition updates and to enforce and actively monitor host compliance (for example: applies relevant schedule/frequency/approach for updates, scanning or verification; alerts if host agent features or agent integrity is corrupted).  13.4.  The Supplier shall ensure security event monitoring operations are established and that they cover, at least the following elements:  a)  Monitoring scope and prerequisites;  b)  Tools used for monitoring and correlation;  c)  Security events covered;  d)  Frequency of monitoring;  e)  Clearly defined roles and responsibilities; and  f)   Escalation matrix.  13.5.  The Supplier shall conduct penetration tests at least on an annual basis on all external or internet facing Supplier Systems that Process SC Group Data.  13.6.  The Supplier shall conduct the vulnerability scanning and assessment on the Supplier's Systems in line with the predefined plan and schedule.   13.7.  The Supplier shall implement controls to prevent the use of unsupported and unauthorised software and hardware.  13.8.  For issues identified by the Supplier as non compliant under clauses 13.5 and 13.6, the Supplier is required to remedy them by:  a)  managing, documenting and tracking the issue to resolution within appropriate timelines for each level of criticality and risk;  b)  tracking the remediation process to completion, after which a rescan/retest must be performed to confirm that the issue has been remediated; and  c)  reporting and escalating to relevant internal stakeholders and committees.     Glossary of terms  For the purpose of this Schedule the following capitalised words shall have the following meanings:  Agreement means the written agreement entered into between the Supplier and a SC Group member for the provision of goods or services.  Applicable Law means laws, rules, regulations, regulatory guidance, regulatory requirements and any form of secondary legislation, resolution, policy, guideline, concession or case law of the relevant jurisdiction from time to time having the force of law and relevant to the provision, receipt or use of the Supplies, including but without limitation to any sanctions laws and other laws relating to anti money laundering, anti bribery and corruption, tax evasion or similar tax crimes, including facilitation and other financial crimes and shall include Data Protection Laws.  Customer Data means any data, records or information (in any form) relating directly or indirectly to a Person that is a customer or client of any SC Group member (including but not limited to past, present or future customers or clients, Supplier guarantors or sureties of any SC Group member) and from which it is practicable for the identity of the Person to be directly or indirectly ascertained by reference to other data, records or information.   Data Protection Laws means all statutes, laws, secondary legislation, regulations, guidelines, and industry standards applicable to SC Group pertaining to privacy, confidentiality, banking secrecy or the protection of Personal Data or Customer Data.  Good Industry Practice means that degree of skill, diligence, care, prudence, foresight and operating systems and practice which would reasonably and ordinarily be expected of a skilled and experienced supplier engaged in the same or a similar type of business as that of the Supplier (within the financial services sector or otherwise) under similar circumstances.  Person means any individual including their personal representatives, sole proprietorship, partnership, corporation, limited liability company, unincorporated society or association, trust or other entity or organisation including the directors and officers of such entity or organisation.  Personal Data means any data, records or information (in any form) relating directly or indirectly to an individual (including but not limited to past, present or future personnel, customers, suppliers, Supplier guarantors or sureties of any SC Group member) and from which it is practicable for the identity of the individual to be directly or indirectly ascertained by reference to other data, records or information and as may be defined under applicable Data Protection Laws in the relevant jurisdiction(s) relating to the origin of the Personal Data Processed under the Agreement.   Processing means any processing operation(s) performed upon SC Group Data whether by automatic means or not, such as collecting, recording, using, accessing, copying, retaining, storing, handling, disclosing, modifying, altering, transferring, transmitting, deleting, or destroying, and Process and Processed have a corresponding meaning.  SC means the SC Group member which the Supplier has entered into an Agreement with.   SC Group means Standard Chartered Bank; and   (a)  any person, body corporate, partnership, firm or other entity in which from time to time Standard Chartered Bank directly or indirectly:  (i)  owns more than half the capital, income, business assets or any other comparable equity or ownership interest;   (ii)  has the power to exercise fifty percent (50%) or more of the voting rights in such entity;   (iii)  has the legal power to direct or cause the direction or general management or affairs of the entity in question;   (iv)  has the power to appoint or remove more than half the members of the supervisory board, board of directors or bodies legally representing such entity; or  (v)  has the right to manage the business of such entity.  (b)  any person, body corporate, partnership, firm or other entity which directly or indirectly has in or over Standard Chartered Bank the rights or powers listed in (a) above ('a Controller'); and  (c)  any person, body corporate, partnership, firm or other entity in which a Controller directly or indirectly has the rights or powers listed in (a) above.  SC Group Data means all proprietary and confidential information of SC or another SC Group member and those of their customers, clients, personnel or suppliers whether commercial, financial, technical or otherwise (whether oral, in writing, machine readable or in any other form) and material (whether electronically recorded, in writing or otherwise) which by its very nature should obviously be treated as secret and confidential or which is designated as such and which SC Group desires to protect against unrestricted disclosure or competitive use, including without limitation:  (a)  Customer Data and Personal Data;  (b)  information relating directly or indirectly to SC Group's business, including but not limited to details of trade secrets, know how, strategies, ideas, operations, compliance information, processes, methodologies and practices;   (c)  information supplied to SC Group by suppliers which SC is authorised to disclose;   (d)  information relating directly or indirectly to SC Group's plans, intentions, know how, market opportunities and business affairs or those of its suppliers, customers (including potential customers) and clients;   (e)  works of authorship, products and materials written and prepared by SC or another SC Group member in relation to the Agreement including but not limited to computer programs, data, diagrams, charts, reports, specifications, sketches, inventions and working papers or similar materials of whatever nature or on whatever media relating thereto;   (f)  any information resulting directly or indirectly from the discussions or negotiations relating to the Agreement and all copies, notes, records and all related information (in any form) generated by SC or another SC Group member based on or arising from any disclosures for the Agreement; and  (g)  the terms of any agreement reached by SC or another SC Group member and the Supplier or proposed by either SC or another SC Group member or the Supplier (whether agreed or not) in connection with the Agreement.  Schedule means this document as updated from time to time.    Services mean the services provided by the Supplier under or in relation to the Agreement.  Standard Chartered Bank means Standard Chartered Bank incorporated in England and Wales with limited liability by Royal Charter 1853 reference number ZC 18.  Supplier means the party providing Services or deliverables to SC or other members of SC Group under or in relation to the Agreement.  Supplier Facilities means the Supplier operation centres, server rooms, wiring closets and other critical infrastructure areas used to provide the Services under the Supplier's or its authorised sub contractors' control and where SC Group Data may be accessed from.  Supplier Staff means the Supplier personnel (including employees, agents, and sub contractors) engaged to provide the Services pursuant to the Agreement.  Supplier Systems means all information technology and communications systems used by the Supplier or made available by the Supplier to SC Group members in the provision of the Services or that may Process SC Group Data, including but not limited to software, databases, firewalls, servers, networks, hardware, middleware, and CPUs.   SC Group Systems means all information technology and communications systems and networks used by SC Group or made available by SC Group to the Supplier and Supplier Staff including but not limited to software, databases, firewalls, servers, hardware, middleware, and CPUs.  Technology Records means all information security related logs for Supplier Systems used to Process SC Group Data, including system access logs, network logs, security logs, application logs, database logs, audit, and event logs.

clauses.description: Material Cyber Security Incidents (Information Security Annexure)  2.7 The Supplier acknowledges that the Customer is obligated to notify Regulatory Authorities of certain Material Cyber Security Incidents, and the Supplier shall promptly inform the Customer of a Material Cyber Security Incident without undue delay, and in any event no later than:  2.7.1 one (1) hour, if the Material Cyber Security Incident is a Relevant Incident; or  2.7.2 twenty four (24) hours for all other Material Cyber Security Incidents,  after the Supplier becomes aware of or reasonably suspects there has been a Material Cyber Security Incident.
