clauses.description: DATA PRIVACY ANNEXURE (JOINT CONTROLLER)  DRAFTING NOTE:   •  THIS TEMPLATE PRIVACY CLAUSE SHOULD BE USED IN VENDOR CONTRACTS WHERE THE PARTIES ACT AS JOINT CONTROLLERS IN RELATION TO THE PERSONAL DATA.  •  THIS TEMPLATE CLAUSE ASSUMES CROSS BORDER DATA TRANSFERS ARE IN SCOPE (OR MAY BE IN THE FUTURE) AND THEREFORE INCLUDES BOTH THE UK AND EU STANDARD CONTRACTUAL CLAUSES FOR TRANSFERS OF UK AND EU PERSONAL DATA TO THIRD COUNTRIES. IF A CROSS BORDER TRANSFER IS NOT IN SCOPE, THE CLAUSE CAN BE SIMPLIFIED. PLEASE SEE THE ALTERNATIVE PRIVACY CLAUSE OPTIONS TEMPLATE FOR DETAILS.  1.  DATA PRIVACY (GDPR)  For the purposes of this paragraph 1, the following capitalised terms shall have the following meanings:  Applicable Laws has the meaning given to it in the Agreement;  Customer Group has the meaning given to it in the Agreement;  Controller Standard Contractual Clauses means the EU Standard Contractual Clauses, Module One, and the UK Addendum (as the context dictates);  Data Breach has the meaning given to it in the Information Security Annexure;   Data Complaint a complaint or request by a third party relating to either Party's obligations under Data Protection Laws in respect (in whole or in part) of Personal Data being processed from time to time in connection with this Agreement, including any actual or threatened compensation claim from a Data Subject or any request for information, notice, investigation or other action from a relevant Data Protection Supervisory Authority;  Data Protection Laws means all Applicable Laws and all statutes, laws, secondary legislation, regulations, guidelines and industry standards applicable to the Customer and/or a member of the Customer Group and/or the Supplier Group pertaining to privacy (including EU GDPR and UK GDPR), confidentiality, banking secrecy and/or the protection of Personal Data, all as from time to time amended, extended, re enacted or consolidated;  Data Subject Request means a request or notice from a Data Subject seeking to exercise their rights under Data Protection Laws in respect of Personal Data, including specific requests from individuals to access Personal Data, rectify inaccuracies, delete Personal Data or restrict the Processing of Personal Data;  Data Transfer Mechanism means any valid personal data transfer safeguard as recognised under applicable Data Protection Laws;  EEA means the European Economic Area which is composed of the Member States of the European Union and the three EEA EFTA States (Iceland, Liechtenstein, and Norway). For the avoidance of doubt, the UK is not a member of the EEA;  EU GDPR means Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27th April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data, and repealing Directive 95/46/EC;  Good Industry Practice means that degree of skill, diligence, care, prudence, foresight and operating systems and practice which would reasonably and ordinarily be expected of a skilled and experienced supplier engaged in the same or a similar type of business as that of the Supplier (as defined under the Agreement and within the financial services sector or otherwise) under similar circumstances;  Information Security Controls means the Information Security Controls under Information Security Annexure;  Joint Controllers has meaning set out in Data Protection Laws;  Joint Controller Activities the processing of Personal Data undertaken by the Parties as Joint Controllers for the Permitted Purpose, as more particularly described in the 'Nature of Processing' section in Schedule 1  EU Standard Contractual Clauses means the agreement in the form annexed to the European Commission's decision of 4 June 2021 on standard contractual clauses for the transfer of personal data in the EU to third countries or the replacement agreement annexed to any subsequent European Commission decision for use in relation to Restricted Transfers from a controller located in the EU/EEA (or otherwise subject to the EU GDPR) to controllers or processors established outside the EU/EEA (and not subject to the EU GDPR) as set out in Schedule 2;  Party means a party to this Agreement, and 'Parties' shall be construed accordingly  Permitted Purpose means the purpose(s) for which the Parties may process Personal Data as Joint Controller[s], as more particularly described in the 'Purpose of Processing' section of Schedule 1; [Drafting note: If the controller obligations are only to apply to the Counterparty, you should refer to the Counterparty here].  Personal Data means Personal Data which is to be processed by the Parties as Joint Controllers under this Agreement, as more particularly described in Schedule 1;  Processing means any processing operation(s) performed upon Personal Data whether by automatic means or not, such as collecting, recording, using, accessing, copying, retaining, storing, handling, disclosing, modifying, altering, transferring, transmitting, deleting or destroying, and Process, Processes and Processed shall have a corresponding meaning;  Regulatory Authority means any regulatory body (including any central bank, government department or agency, law enforcement authority or other authority in any part of the world) which has the responsibility of supervising or regulating banks and other financial institutions generally, including the Customer Group and any branch or representative office of the Customer Group;  Restricted Transfer means a transfer of Personal Data which is undergoing processing or which is intended to be processed after transfer, to a Third Country and/or for which there is a requirement to take additional steps to adequately protect the Personal Data in order for the transfer to be lawful under the Data Protection Laws;  Subcontractor means any permitted person engaged by the Supplier to perform any part of the work under this Agreement OR the relevant Order Form for the Supplier, and includes consultants, subcontractors, suppliers and other contractors  Supplier has the meaning given to it in the Agreement;  Third Country means a country, territory or organisation that is not recognised under Data Protection Laws as providing adequate protection for Personal Data  Sub processor means any subcontractor who is or will be Processing Personal Data on behalf of a Supplier in relation to the Agreement;    UK means the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland;  UK Addendum  means the International Data Transfer Addendum to the EU Commission Standard Contractual Clauses (Version B.1.0) issued by the Information Commissioner's Office and laid before Parliament in accordance with section 119A of the Data Protection Act 2018 on 28 January 2022, as it is revised under Section 18 of Part 2: Mandatory Clauses of that document as set out in Schedule 3;   UK GDPR means Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27th April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data (General Data Protection Regulation) as it forms part of the laws of England and Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland by virtue of section 3 of the European Union (Withdrawal) Act 2018, and for the avoidance of doubt, includes any amendment made to it by the Data Protection, Privacy and Electronic Communications (Amendments etc) (EU Exit) Regulations 2019.  [DRAFTING NOTE: DEPENDING ON THE AGREEMENT THESE PROVISIONS ARE BEING INSERTED INTO, IT MAY BE APPROPRIATE TO REPLACE THE REFERENCES TO 'PERSONAL DATA' WITH A REFERENCE TO A BROADER CATEGORY OF DATA.  HOWEVER, THESE TERMS ARE PRESCRIPTIVE REQUIREMENTS OF DATA PROTECTION LAWS AND THIRD PARTIES MAY PUSH BACK ON APPLYING ALL THE PROVISIONS TO ALL CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION. FOR EXAMPLE, IN THE BANK'S STANDARD TEMPLATES, IT MAY BE APPROPRIATE TO REFER TO 'CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION', WHICH THE BANK TYPICALLY DEFINES TO INCLUDE PERSONAL DATA AND BANK CLIENT DATA. AN APPROPRIATE DEFINITION FOR 'APPLICABLE LAWS' WILL NEED TO BE INCLUDED IN THE DEFINITIONS.]  1.1  Unless the context otherwise requires, capitalised terms used but not defined in this Schedule shall have the meaning given to them in the Agreement.  1.2  Where the Parties are Processing (or procuring the Processing of) Personal Data for the Permitted Purpose, the Parties agree that they shall be Joint Controllers and each be responsible for compliance with the obligations imposed on it as Joint Controller under Data Protection Laws.  1.3  The Parties agree that they will each:  (a)  ensure that the Personal Data is only used for the Permitted Purpose and not for any other purpose(s);  (b)  ensure that they have a lawful basis for the Joint Controller Activities and the sharing of the Personal Data with the other Party for the Permitted Purpose;  (c)  where necessary for the Permitted Purpose or as required by Data Protection Laws, carry out a data protection impact assessment (as defined under Data Protection Laws);  (d)  provide fair notice to relevant Data Subjects in respect of the Joint Controller Activities in accordance with Data Protection Laws;  (e)  implement, and assist the other Party to implement, appropriate technical and organisational security measures at a minimum to the level set out in the Information Security Controls to ensure a level of security appropriate to the risk presented by Processing the Personal Data, in particular from a Data Breach. For the avoidance of doubt, the Supplier shall comply with the Information Security Controls (as defined under the Agreement) at all times;   [DRAFTING NOTE: IN RELATION TO PARAGRAPH 1.4(e): IF THERE IS NO INFORMATION SECURITY ANNEXURE IN THE APPLICABLE CONTRACT, YOU SHOULD INCLUDE THE ALTERNATIVE PARAGRAPH 1.4(e) FROM THE ALTERNATIVE PRIVACY CLAUSE OPTIONS TEMPLATE.]  (f)  ensure that all Processors of Personal Data are appointed with the prior written consent of the other Party and in accordance with Data Protection Laws;   (g)  maintain records of compliance with their respective responsibilities under this paragraph 1.4 and Data Protection Laws; and   (h)  when the parties disclose  Personal Data for the Permitted Purpose, each Party shall do so in the form, format and on the dates and at the intervals as agreed between the Parties and in accordance with appropriate information governance arrangements.  1.4  Each Party shall  notify the other Party in the event of a Data Breach (whether suspected or actual) as soon as it becomes aware of it.  1.5  The Supplier shall respond to any Data Breach in accordance with the terms of the Agreement including paragraphs 6.9 to 6.11 of the Regulatory Schedule of the Agreement.  [DRAFTING NOTE: THE DATA BREACH PROVISIONS ARE CONTAINED IN THE MATERIAL CYBER SECURITY INCIDENT SECTION OF THE INFORMATION SECURITY ANNEXURE. IF THE MATERIAL CYBER SECURITY INCIDENT PROVISIONS ARE NOT REQUIRED FOR THE VENDOR CONTRACT YOU ARE WORKING ON, YOU MUST INCLUDE THE ALTERNATIVE DATA BREACH CLAUSE OPTION INSTEAD. SEE THE ALTERNATIVE PARAGRAPH 1.13(d) FROM THE ALTERNATIVE PRIVACY CLAUSE OPTIONS TEMPLATE.]  1.6  The Parties shall consult with and assist each other in relation to any required notification to any Data Protection Supervisory Authority and (if applicable) to Data Subjects in respect of a Data Breach, provided always that, in the absence of agreement further to a reasonable period of consultation, the Customer will determine if a Data Breach should be notified in accordance with this paragraph 1.7 (as well as the content of any such notification) and the Customer shall provide such notifications to such Data Protection Supervisory Authority and (if applicable) Data Subjects. [DRAFTING NOTE: IF APPROPRIATE REFER TO THE DISPUTE RESOLUTION PROCEDURE IN THE AGREEMENT]  1.7  Without prejudice to the co operation provisions at paragraph 1.6 and paragraph 1.7 above, the [Supplier] will not make or permit any announcement, notification or report to be made or given to any person in respect of a Data Breach without the prior written consent of the Customer (not to be unreasonably withheld), provided always that where the [Supplier] is required to make such an announcement, notification or report in order to comply with the Data Protection Laws or any other applicable laws, the Customer's consent under this paragraph 1.8 shall not be required and instead the [Supplier] shall notify the Customer in writing prior to such announcement (unless it is prohibited from doing so by applicable laws).   1.8  The Parties agree that they will act as Joint Controllers in respect of any Data Complaint received in relation to the Joint Controller Activities. The Party which receives the Data Complaint shall notify the other Party promptly of its receipt. The Parties will then discuss the Data Complaint promptly and in good faith to agree how such Data Complaint should be dealt with. If the Parties fail to agree the course of action which should be taken within seven (7) days of the Data Complaint having been received (or such shorter period as may be required by the Data Complaint), the Customer (acting reasonably) shall determine the action to be taken in response in its sole discretion.   1.9  Each Party will notify the other Party promptly and without undue delay if it receives a Data Subject Request in respect of Joint Controller Activities. The Party that receives the Data Subject Request shall be responsible for responding to such request, provided that the other Party will provide reasonable assistance to the Party in receipt of such request in responding to the Data Subject Request. The Parties acknowledge and agree that relevant Data Subjects may exercise their rights to make a Data Subject Request in relation to Personal Data in respect of and against either Party.   [DRAFTING NOTE: THE TEMPLATE DOES NOT INCLUDE ANY SPECIFIC LIABILITY PROVISIONS WITHIN THIS CLAUSE. ALTHOUGH CONTROLLERS ARE RESPONSIBLE FOR THEIR OWN COMPLIANCE WITH DATA PROTECTION LAW OBLIGATIONS, CONSIDER ADDING SOME PROVISIONS TO DEAL WITH LIABILITY ARISING FROM DATA SUBJECT RIGHTS REQUESTS ETC, IF APPLICABLE. INDIVIDUALS CAN SEEK COMPENSATION FROM JOINT CONTROLLERS AND EACH JOINT CONTROLLER WILL BE LIABLE FOR THE ENTIRE DAMAGE CAUSED BY THE PROCESSING, UNLESS IT CAN PROVE IT IS NOT IN ANY WAY RESPONSIBLE FOR THE EVENT GIVING RISE TO THE DAMAGE    SCB MAY BE ABLE TO CLAIM BACK FROM ANOTHER CONTROLLER OR PROCESSOR THE SHARE OF THE COMPENSATION FOR WHICH SCB IS LIABLE. PLEASE CHECK THE GENERAL LIABILITY PROVISIONS OF THE CONTRACT FOR SUITABILITY.]  1.10  The parties further agree to comply with their respective obligations as  'data importer' or ‘data exporter' in the relevant Controller Standard Contractual Clauses.  Subcontracting/Sub processing  1.11  The [Supplier] shall be authorised to share Personal Data where necessary to achieve the Agreed Purpose with:   (a)  Sub processors, subject to the [Supplier] entering into a prior written agreement with such Sub processors which contains obligations which are equivalent to the obligations set out in this Paragraph 1 [and obligations imposed on processors under Article 28 of the EU GDPR] (or its equivalent provision under the UK GDPR if applicable); and/or  (a)  Other third party recipients who are controllers (as defined under the EU GDPR and/or the UK GDPR as applicable) subject to the [Supplier] entering into a prior written agreement with such third parties which contains obligations which are equivalent to the obligations set out in this Paragraph 1;   1.12  The [Supplier] shall remain liable to the Customer for the acts and/or omissions of such Sub processors or other third party recipients as if they were the acts and/or omissions of the [Supplier].  Third Country Access or Transfer  [DRAFTING NOTE:   •  THE FOLLOWING PROVISIONS DEAL WITH THIRD COUNTRY ACCESS OR TRANSFER OF PERSONAL DATA. THE DRAFTING ASSUMES BOTH A UK AND EU DATA TRANSFER/REMOTE ACCESS IS APPLICABLE. IT IS RECOMMENDED TO INCLUDE THIS LONG FORM APPROACH EVEN IF SUCH THIRD COUNTRY ACCESS/TRANSFER IS NOT APPLICABLE ON DAY 1 IN CASE IT SUBSEQUENTLY BECOMES REQUIRED. THIS AVOIDS THE NEED FOR A CONTRACT VARIATION / ADDENDUM AT THAT POINT IN TIME.   •  THIS TEMPLATE CLAUSE INCLUDES THE CONTROLLER TO CONTROLLER STANDARD CONTRACTUAL CLAUSES ON A BINDING BASIS IN THE CONTRACT. THE CONTROLLER SCCs ARE PART OF THIS TEMPLATE AND MUST BE INCLUDED AS A SCHEDULE TO THE CONTRACT. IF YOU ARE WORKING ON VENDOR / THIRD PARTY PAPER, PLEASE ADD THESE CLAUSES AND THE SCHEDULES TO THE CONTRACT. THE SCC SCHEDULE CANNOT BE AMENDED BY THE PARTIES SAVE FOR SELECTING THE LIMITED OPTIONS WITHIN THE SCCs AND COMPLETING THE PLACEHOLDERS FOR INFORMATION.  •  IN THE MAJORITY OF CASES, THE CONTROLLER TO CONTROLLER STANDARD CONTRACTUAL CLAUSES MAY BE USED IN A JOINT CONTROLLER SCENARIO. THIS IS NOT A COMMON SCENARIO. THE USE OF THE CONTROLLER SCCs FOR A JOINT CONTROLLER ARRANGEMENT HAS NOT BEEN TESTED IN THE COURTS AND THERE IS NO OFFICIAL GUIDANCE ON THIS POINT. CONSIDER WHETHER ANY ADDITIONAL OBLIGATIONS ARE REQUIRED FOR THE PARTICULAR CONTRACT AND THE PRIVACY RISK. FURTHER GUIDANCE IS SET OUT IN THE SCCS PLAYBOOK.   •  IF THE VENDOR / THIRD PARTY DOES NOT AGREE TO THIS APPROACH AND/OR IF THIRD COUNTRY ACCESS / TRANSFER OF PERSONAL DATA IS NOT FORESEEABLE AS PART OF THE ARRANGEMENT, YOU CAN USE THE SHORTER FORM ‘OPTION B' CLAUSES. PLEASE SEE THE ALTERNATIVE PRIVACY CLAUSE OPTIONS TEMPLATE FOR DETAILS.]  1.13  Notwithstanding anything to the contrary in this [Agreement]/[Order Form], the [Supplier] shall not, without having obtained the Customer's or the relevant member(s) of the Customer Group's prior written consent in each case, or written authorisation set out in the [Agreement], transfer (or accept receipt or of or allow access to) Personal Data to a Third Country. Such access or transfer permitted by the Customer or a member of the Customer Group pursuant to this paragraph 1.14 shall be subject to an additional security and legal and compliance review by the Customer Group and such additional restrictions or terms that the Customer or relevant member of the Customer Group may reasonably require to comply with Data Protection Laws.  1.14  Where the [Supplier] is required by Applicable Law to which it is subject to disclose or transfer the Personal Data processed under the Agreement to a Third Country, and provided that the disclosure, transfer or remote access is permitted under the laws of the jurisdiction from/to which the disclosure, remote access or transfer is proposed to take place, the Supplier shall inform the Customer Group in writing of such legal requirement before disclosing, transferring or permitting access to the Personal Data, unless Applicable Law prohibits such disclosure to the Customer or the relevant member of the Customer Group on important grounds of public interest.  1.15  Any Restricted Transfers pursuant to this Agreement (including to Sub processors) shall be made in compliance with Data Protection Laws and use the relevant Controller Standard Contractual Clauses or other applicable Data Transfer Mechanism (as requested by the Customer from time to time). Prior to making any Restricted Transfer, the [Supplier] shall notify the Customer of the name and location (territory or country) of the recipient, as well as the reason and appropriate Data Transfer Mechanism in place for the Restricted Transfer.   1.16  The [Supplier] acknowledges that the Customer has authority, on behalf of itself and each relevant member of the Customer Group involved in the Processing of Personal Data under or in connection with this Agreement and acting as a controller or exporter, to enter into and execute the EU Standard Contractual Clauses, the UK Addendum and any other valid data transfer safeguard set out in this [Agreement] (as applicable) and as recognised under a Data Transfer Mechanism.  [DRAFTING NOTE: THIS PARAGRAPH 1.17 IS INCLUDED TO ALLOW THE CONTROLLER SCCs TO BE ENTERED INTO AT THE GTC LEVEL.]  1.17  The [Supplier] acknowledges that, in the event it transfers Personal Data pursuant to this Agreement (including to its Sub processors), it will undertake any corresponding data transfer impact assessment or a third party privacy assessment so as to identify and implement any proportionate technical and operation safeguards (including privacy by design and by default measures, where necessary) to protect against the loss, disclosure or access of any Personal Data in accordance with this paragraph 1 and as required under Data Protection Laws.   EU and UK International Personal Data transfers   1.18  Any Restricted Transfers pursuant to this Agreement which are subject to the EU GDPR and/or the UK Addendum shall be governed by the EU Standard Contractual Clauses and/or the UK Addendum as set out in Schedule 2 and 3.  1.19  The [Supplier] will enter into and execute any other appropriate documentation and safeguards in compliance with this paragraph 1.19 and with applicable Data Protection Laws or as requested by the Customer from time to time.  1.20  If, in the performance of the [Agreement], the [Supplier] transfers any  Personal Data to a Sub processor located in, or permits Processing of any Personal Data by a Sub processor from a Third Country, then: (i)  the [Supplier shall enter the same EU Standard Contractual Clauses and/or the UK Addendum with the Sub processor(s) (where applicable) or (ii) upon request by the Customer, the Supplier shall procure that the Sub processor will enter into the relevant EU Standard Contractual Clauses and/or the UK Addendum with the Customer (acting for itself and for the relevant member(s) of the Customer Group who is (are) controllers) or such other Data Transfer Mechanism under Data Protection Laws, as appropriate.  Change of statutory transfer mechanism and risk assessments  1.21  To the extent that the [Supplier] and the Customer (and the relevant member(s) of the Customer Group) are relying on the Controller Standard Contractual Clauses or other applicable Data Transfer Mechanism and those mechanisms are subsequently modified, revoked, or held in a court of competent jurisdiction to be invalid, the Customer (and the relevant member(s) of the Customer Group) and the [Supplier] agree to cooperate in good faith to promptly suspend the transfer or to pursue a suitable alternate mechanism that can lawfully support the transfer.  1.22  To the extent that the [Supplier] and the Customer (and the relevant member(s) of the Customer Group) are relying on the Controller Standard Contractual Clauses or other applicable Data Transfer Mechanism and where in the Customer's reasonable opinion, risk assessments are necessary in respect of the same, the [Supplier] will upon request promptly provide reasonable assistance and cooperation to the Customer (at the Supplier's own costs) with regard to the carrying out of risk assessments in order to enable the Customer (and the relevant member(s) of the Customer Group who is (are) controllers) to legitimise the international data transfers in accordance with Data Protection Laws, Good Industry Practice and the Customer's instructions.  1.23  This paragraph 1 shall survive the termination or expiry of the Agreement          SCHEDULE 1  Personal Data  Subject matter of processing  [DETAILS]    [Give an overview/summary of the subject matter of the contract   this is the general reason/purpose for the agreement]  Duration of processing  [DETAILS]    [Set out here the term of the processing of personal data under the agreement].  Nature of processing and joint controller roles and responsibilities     [DETAILS]    [Specify the types of processing activities that will be carried out on the personal data  e.g. storage, sharing, reviewing, reporting, back up, recovery, analysis etc.  This section should set out what will or could happen to the personal data under the Agreement. This is an expansion of the 'subject matter' given above. You should ensure that all the activities you will undertake as Joint Controllers using personal data are described here. You should set out each party's roles and responsibilities for complying with the UK and/or EU GDPR. ]  Purpose of processing  [DETAILS]    [This should describe why the personal data is to be processed.  There is an element of overlap with the subject matter and nature of processing information, but this should be geared towards explaining what the outcome of the processing will be, e.g.: [For the purpose of [e.g. [data management][delivery of [X] services]] [ensuring [X issue] can be managed and dealt with promptly and efficiently']]. It should be clear from this description what exactly the Joint Controllers can and will do with the personal data (and what the limits of its processing under the Agreement are).   Type of Personal Data  [DETAILS]    [Specify the categories of personal data that will be processed under the Agreement, e.g.: name, address, age, education details, work related information, preferences etc.   Note explicitly if there are any special categories of personal data that will be processed by the Joint Controllers.  Categories of Data Subject   [DETAILS]    [Set out the categories of data subjects whose personal data will be processed under the agreement e.g.: The Customer's or Joint Controller's customers, employees (specify if contractors, temporary/permanent employees/workers etc and/or hired consultants are included) and/or customers, employees and/or hired consultants etc of partners and suppliers to The Customer/Joint Controller.     Responding to individuals' rights request   [DETAILS]  [Please explain how each Joint Controller will comply with its transparency obligations and individuals' rights. You may choose to specify a central point of contact for individuals.]              SCHEDULE 2    EU STANDARD CONTRACTUAL CLAUSES      EU Standard Contractual Clauses for the transfer of personal data to third countries pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2016/679    (Module 1   Controller to Controller Transfers)      SECTION 1   1.  PURPOSE AND SCOPE  (a)  The purpose of these standard contractual clauses is to ensure compliance with the requirements of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 27 April 2016 on the protection of natural persons with regard to the processing of personal data and on the free movement of such data (General Data Protection Regulation)  for the transfer of personal data to a third country.  (b)  The Parties:  (i)  the natural or legal person(s), public authority/ies, agency/ies or other body/ies (hereinafter ‘entity/ies') transferring the personal data, as listed in Annex 1. (hereinafter each ‘Data Exporter'); and  (ii)  the entity/ies in a third country receiving the personal data from the Data Exporter, directly or indirectly via another entity also Party to these clauses, as listed in Annex 1. (hereinafter each ‘Data Importer')  have agreed to these standard contractual clauses (hereinafter: ‘clauses').  (c)  These clauses apply with respect to the transfer of personal data as specified in Annex 1.  (d)  The Appendix to these clauses containing the Annexes referred to therein forms an integral part of these clauses.  2.  EFFECT AND INVARIABILITY OF THE CLAUSES  (a)  These clauses set out appropriate safeguards, including enforceable data subject rights and effective legal remedies, pursuant to Article 46(1) and Article 46(2)(c) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 and, with respect to data transfers from controllers to processors and/or processors to processors, standard contractual clauses pursuant to Article 28(7) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679, provided they are not modified, except to select the appropriate Module(s) or to add or update information in the Appendix. This does not prevent the Parties from including the standard contractual clauses laid down in these clauses in a wider contract and/or to add other clauses or additional safeguards, provided that they do not contradict, directly or indirectly, these clauses or prejudice the fundamental rights or freedoms of data subjects.  (b)  These clauses are without prejudice to obligations to which the Data Exporter is subject by virtue of Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  3.  THIRD PARTY BENEFICIARIES  (a)  Data subjects may invoke and enforce these clauses, as third party beneficiaries, against the Data Exporter and/or Data Importer, with the following exceptions:  (i)  clause 1, clause 2, clause 3, clause 6, clause 7];  (ii)  clause  8.5(e) and 8.9(b);   (iii)  clause 9   (iv)  clause 12(a) and 12(d);   (v)  clause 13;   (vi)  clause: 15.1(c), 15.1(d) and 15.1(e);   (vii)  clause 16(e);  (viii)  clause  18(a) and 18(b);   (b)  Paragraph 3(a) is without prejudice to rights of data subjects under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  4.  INTERPRETATION  (a)  Where these clauses use terms that are defined in Regulation (EU) 2016/679, those terms shall have the same meaning as in that Regulation.  (b)  These clauses shall be read and interpreted in the light of the provisions of Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  (c)  These clauses shall not be interpreted in a way that conflicts with rights and obligations provided for in Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  5.  HIERARCHY  In the event of a contradiction between these clauses and the provisions of related agreements between the Parties, existing at the time these clauses are agreed or entered into thereafter, these clauses shall prevail.  6.  DESCRIPTION OF THE TRANSFER(S)  The details of the transfer(s), and in particular the categories of personal data that are transferred and the purpose(s) for which they are transferred, are specified in Annex 1.  7.  [OPTIONAL: DOCKING CLAUSE  [DRAFTING NOTE   •  AS A STARTING POSITION, IT IS ASSUMED THAT THE DOCKING CLAUSE WILL IN MOST CASES NOT BE RELEVANT/APPLICABLE IN WHICH CASE YOU SHOULD PUT 'OPTIONAL CLAUSE REMOVED'.   •  IF THIS IS REQUESTED ON THE BASIS THE THIRD PARTY BELIEVES IT IS REQUIRED TO REFLECT THE NATURE OF THE ARRANGEMENT, THIS MUST BE CONFIRMED WITH THE FIRST LINE CONTRACT OWNER (WITH INPUT FROM PRIVACY OPERATIONS), WHO IS RESPONSIBLE FOR MAKING THIS DECISION. THE FIRST LINE CONTRACT OWNER MAY NEED TO UPDATE ITS RESPONSES TO THE TPPA PROCESS AND OTHER PRIVACY RISK ASSESSMENTS MAY BE REQUIRED.   •  PLEASE CONSULT THE SCC PLAYBOOK FOR FURTHER GUIDANCE AND, IF YOU HAVE ANY QUERIES, PLEASE CONTACT ENTERPRISE LEGAL AT: DATAPRIVACY.LEGALQUERIES@SC.COM  FOR MORE GUIDANCE.]  (a)  An entity that is not a Party to these clauses may, with the agreement of the Parties, accede to these clauses at any time, either as a Data Exporter or as a Data Importer, by completing the Appendix and signing Annex 1.  (b)  Once it has completed the Appendix and signed Annex 1., the acceding entity shall become a Party to these clauses and have the rights and obligations of a Data Exporter or Data Importer in accordance with its designation in Annex 1.  (c)  The acceding entity shall have no rights or obligations arising under these clauses from the period prior to becoming a Party].  OR  [OPTIONAL CLAUSE REMOVED]   SECTION 2 OBLIGATIONS OF THE PARTIES  8.  DATA PROTECTION SAFEGUARDS  The Data Exporter warrants that it has used reasonable efforts to determine that the Data Importer is able, through the implementation of appropriate technical and organisational measures, to satisfy its obligations under these clauses.  8.1  Purpose limitation  The Data Importer shall process the personal data only for the specific purpose(s) of the transfer, as set out in Annex 1. It may only process the personal data for another purpose:  (i)  where it has obtained the data subject's prior consent;  (ii)  where necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims in the context of specific administrative, regulatory or judicial proceedings; or  (iii)  where necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person.    8.2  Transparency  (a)  In order to enable data subjects to effectively exercise their rights pursuant to clause 10, the Data Importer shall inform them, either directly or through the Data Exporter:  (i)  of its identity and contact details;  (ii)  of the categories of personal data processed;  (iii)  of the right to obtain a copy of these clauses;  (iv)  where it intends to onward transfer the personal data to any third party/ies, of the recipient or categories of recipients (as appropriate with a view to providing meaningful information), the purpose of such onward transfer and the ground therefore pursuant to clause 8.7.  (b)  Paragraph 8.2(a) shall not apply where the data subject already has the information, including when such information has already been provided by the Data Exporter, or providing the information proves impossible or would involve a disproportionate effort for the Data Importer. In the latter case, the Data Importer shall, to the extent possible, make the information publicly available.  (c)  On request, the Parties shall make a copy of these clauses, including the Appendix as completed by them, available to the data subject free of charge. To the extent necessary to protect business secrets or other confidential information, including personal data, the Parties may redact part of the text of the Appendix prior to sharing a copy, but shall provide a meaningful summary where the data subject would otherwise not be able to understand its content or exercise his/her rights. On request, the Parties shall provide the data subject with the reasons for the redactions, to the extent possible without revealing the redacted information.  (d)  Paragraphs 8.2(a) 8.2(b) to 8.2(c) are without prejudice to the obligations of the Data Exporter under Articles 13 and 14 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  8.3  Accuracy and data minimisation  (a)  Each Party shall ensure that the personal data is accurate and, where necessary, kept up to date. The Data Importer shall take every reasonable step to ensure that personal data that is inaccurate, having regard to the purpose(s) of processing, is erased or rectified without delay.  (b)  If one of the Parties becomes aware that the personal data it has transferred or received is inaccurate, or has become outdated, it shall inform the other Party without undue delay.  (c)  The Data Importer shall ensure that the personal data is adequate, relevant and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purpose(s) of processing.   8.4  Storage limitation  The Data Importer shall retain the personal data for no longer than necessary for the purpose(s) for which it is processed. It shall put in place appropriate technical or organisational measures to ensure compliance with this obligation, including erasure or anonymisation  of the data and all back ups at the end of the retention period.  8.5  Security of processing  (a)  The Data Importer and, during transmission, also the Data Exporter shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures to ensure the security of the personal data, including protection against a breach of security leading to accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorised disclosure or access (hereinafter ‘personal data breach'). In assessing the appropriate level of security, they shall take due account of the state of the art, the costs of implementation, the nature, scope, context and purpose(s) of processing and the risks involved in the processing for the data subject. The Parties shall in particular consider having recourse to encryption or pseudonymisation, including during transmission, where the purpose of processing can be fulfilled in that manner.  (b)  The Parties have agreed on the technical and organisational measures set out in Annex 2. The Data Importer shall carry out regular checks to ensure that these measures continue to provide an appropriate level of security.  (c)  The Data Importer shall ensure that persons authorised to process the personal data have committed themselves to confidentiality or are under an appropriate statutory obligation of confidentiality.  (d)  In the event of a personal data breach concerning personal data processed by the Data Importer under these clauses, the Data Importer shall take appropriate measures to address the personal data breach, including measures to mitigate its possible adverse effects.  (e)  In case of a personal data breach that is likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Data Importer shall without undue delay notify both the Data Exporter and the competent supervisory authority pursuant to clause 13. Such notification shall contain i) a description of the nature of the breach (including, where possible, categories and approximate number of data subjects and personal data records concerned), ii) its likely consequences, iii) the measures taken or proposed to address the breach, and iv) the details of a contact point from whom more information can be obtained. To the extent it is not possible for the Data Importer to provide all the information at the same time, it may do so in phases without undue further delay.  (f)  In case of a personal data breach that is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of natural persons, the Data Importer shall also notify without undue delay the data subjects concerned of the personal data breach and its nature, if necessary in cooperation with the Data Exporter, together with the information referred to in paragraph (e), points ii) to iv), unless the Data Importer has implemented measures to significantly reduce the risk to the rights or freedoms of natural persons, or notification would involve disproportionate efforts. In the latter case, the Data Importer shall instead issue a public communication or take a similar measure to inform the public of the personal data breach.  (g)  The Data Importer shall document all relevant facts relating to the personal data breach, including its effects and any remedial action taken, and keep a record thereof.  8.6  Sensitive data  Where the transfer involves personal data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious or philosophical beliefs, or trade union membership, genetic data, or biometric data for the purpose of uniquely identifying a natural person, data concerning health or a person's sex life or sexual orientation, or data relating to criminal convictions or offences (hereinafter ‘sensitive data'), the Data Importer shall apply specific restrictions and/or additional safeguards adapted to the specific nature of the data and the risks involved. This may include restricting the personnel permitted to access the personal data, additional security measures (such as pseudonymisation) and/or additional restrictions with respect to further disclosure.  8.7  Onward transfers  (a)  The Data Importer shall not disclose the personal data to a third party located outside the European Union  (in the same country as the Data Importer or in another third country, hereinafter ‘onward transfer') unless the third party is or agrees to be bound by these clauses, under the appropriate Module. Otherwise, an onward transfer by the Data Importer may only take place if:  (i)  it is to a country benefitting from an adequacy decision pursuant to Article 45 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 that covers the onward transfer;  (ii)  the third party otherwise ensures appropriate safeguards pursuant to Articles 46 or 47 of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 with respect to the processing in question;  (iii)  the third party enters into a binding instrument with the Data Importer ensuring the same level of data protection as under these clauses, and the Data Importer provides a copy of these safeguards to the Data Exporter;  (iv)  it is necessary for the establishment, exercise or defence of legal claims in the context of specific administrative, regulatory or judicial proceedings;  (v)  it is necessary in order to protect the vital interests of the data subject or of another natural person; or  (vi)  where none of the other conditions apply, the Data Importer has obtained the explicit consent of the data subject for an onward transfer in a specific situation, after having informed him/her of its purpose(s), the identity of the recipient and the possible risks of such transfer to him/her due to the lack of appropriate data protection safeguards. In this case, the Data Importer shall inform the Data Exporter and, at the request of the latter, shall transmit to it a copy of the information provided to the data subject.  Any onward transfer is subject to compliance by the Data Importer with all the other safeguards under these clauses, in particular purpose limitation.  8.8  Processing under the authority of the Data Importer  The Data Importer shall ensure that any person acting under its authority, including a processor, processes the data only on its instructions.  8.9  Documentation and compliance  (a)  Each Party shall be able to demonstrate compliance with its obligations under these clauses. In particular, the Data Importer shall keep appropriate documentation of the processing activities carried out under its responsibility.  (b)  The Data Importer shall make such documentation available to the competent supervisory authority on request.  9.  USE OF SUB PROCESSORS   NOT USED  10.  DATA SUBJECT RIGHTS  (a)  The Data Importer, where relevant with the assistance of the Data Exporter, shall deal with any enquiries and requests it receives from a data subject relating to the processing of his/her personal data and the exercise of his/her rights under these clauses without undue delay and at the latest within one month of the receipt of the enquiry or request . The Data Importer shall take appropriate measures to facilitate such enquiries, requests and the exercise of data subject rights. Any information provided to the data subject shall be in an intelligible and easily accessible form, using clear and plain language.  (b)  In particular, upon request by the data subject the Data Importer shall, free of charge:  (i)  provide confirmation to the data subject as to whether personal data concerning him/her is being processed and, where this is the case, a copy of the data relating to him/her and the information; if personal data has been or will be onward transferred, provide information on recipients or categories of recipients (as appropriate with a view to providing meaningful information) to which the personal data has been or will be onward transferred, the purpose of such onward transfers and their ground pursuant to clause 8.7; and provide information on the right to lodge a complaint with a supervisory authority in accordance with clause 11(c)(i);  (ii)  rectify inaccurate or incomplete data concerning the data subject;  (iii)  erase personal data concerning the data subject if such data is being or has been processed in violation of any of these clauses ensuring third party beneficiary rights, or if the data subject withdraws the consent on which the processing is based.  (c)  Where the Data Importer processes the personal data for direct marketing purposes, it shall cease processing for such purposes if the data subject objects to it.  (d)  The Data Importer shall not make a decision based solely on the automated processing of the personal data transferred (hereinafter ‘automated decision'), which would produce legal effects concerning the data subject or similarly significantly affect him/her, unless with the explicit consent of the data subject or if authorised to do so under the laws of the country of destination, provided that such laws lays down suitable measures to safeguard the data subject's rights and legitimate interests. In this case, the Data Importer shall, where necessary in cooperation with the Data Exporter:  (i)  inform the data subject about the envisaged automated decision, the envisaged consequences and the logic involved; and  (ii)  implement suitable safeguards, at least by enabling the data subject to contest the decision, express his/her point of view and obtain review by a human being.  (e)  Where requests from a data subject are excessive, in particular because of their repetitive character, the Data Importer may either charge a reasonable fee taking into account the administrative costs of granting the request or refuse to act on the request.  (f)  The Data Importer may refuse a data subject's request if such refusal is allowed under the laws of the country of destination and is necessary and proportionate in a democratic society to protect one of the objectives listed in Article 23(1) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  (g)  If the Data Importer intends to refuse a data subject's request, it shall inform the data subject of the reasons for the refusal and the possibility of lodging a complaint with the competent supervisory authority and/or seeking judicial redress.  11. The Parties shall keep each other informed about such disputes and, where appropriate, cooperate in resolving them.  (c)  Where the data subject invokes a third party beneficiary right pursuant to clause 3, the Data Importer shall accept the decision of the data subject to:  (i)  lodge a complaint with the supervisory authority in the Member State of his/her habitual residence or place of work, or the competent supervisory authority pursuant to clause 13;  (ii)  refer the dispute to the competent courts within the meaning of clause 18.  (d)  The Parties accept that the data subject may be represented by a not for profit body, organisation or association under the conditions set out in Article 80(1) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  (e)  The Data Importer shall abide by a decision that is binding under the applicable EU or Member State law.  (f)  The Data Importer agrees that the choice made by the data subject will not prejudice his/her substantive and procedural rights to seek remedies in accordance with applicable laws.  12.  LIABILITY  (a)  Each Party shall be liable to the other Party/ies for any damages it causes the other Party/ies by any breach of these clauses.  (b)  Each Party shall be liable to the data subject, and the data subject shall be entitled to receive compensation, for any material or non material damages that the Party causes the data subject by breaching the third party beneficiary rights under these clauses. This is without prejudice to the liability of the Data Exporter under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  (c)  Where more than one Party is responsible for any damage caused to the data subject as a result of a breach of these clauses, all responsible Parties shall be jointly and severally liable and the data subject is entitled to bring an action in court against any of these Parties.  (d)  The Parties agree that if one Party is held liable under paragraph (c), it shall be entitled to claim back from the other Party/ies that part of the compensation corresponding to its/their responsibility for the damage.  (e)  The Data Importer may not invoke the conduct of a processor or sub processor to avoid its own liability.  13.  SUPERVISION  (a)  [Where the Data Exporter is established in an EU Member State:] The supervisory authority with responsibility for ensuring compliance by the Data Exporter with Regulation (EU) 2016/679 as regards the data transfer, as indicated in Annex 1., shall act as competent supervisory authority.  [Where the Data Exporter is not established in an EU Member State, but falls within the territorial scope of application of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 in accordance with its Article 3(2) and has appointed a representative pursuant to Article 27(1) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679:] The supervisory authority of the Member State in which the representative within the meaning of Article 27(1) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 is established, as indicated in Annex 1.C, shall act as competent supervisory authority.  [Where the Data Exporter is not established in an EU Member State, but falls within the territorial scope of application of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 in accordance with its Article 3(2) without however having to appoint a representative pursuant to Article 27(2) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679:] The supervisory authority of one of the Member States in which the data subjects whose personal data is transferred under these clauses in relation to the offering of goods or services to them, or whose behaviour is monitored, are located, as indicated in Annex 1., shall act as competent supervisory authority.  (b)  The Data Importer agrees to submit itself to the jurisdiction of and cooperate with the competent supervisory authority in any procedures aimed at ensuring compliance with these clauses. In particular, the Data Importer agrees to respond to enquiries, submit to audits and comply with the measures adopted by the supervisory authority, including remedial and compensatory measures. It shall provide the supervisory authority with written confirmation that the necessary actions have been taken.  SECTION 3 LOCAL LAWS AND OBLIGATIONS IN CASE OF ACCESS BY PUBLIC AUTHORITIES  14.  LOCAL LAWS AND PRACTICES AFFECTING COMPLIANCE WITH THE CLAUSES  (a)  The Parties warrant that they have no reason to believe that the laws and practices in the third country of destination applicable to the processing of the personal data by the Data Importer, including any requirements to disclose personal data or measures authorising access by public authorities, prevent the Data Importer from fulfilling its obligations under these clauses. This is based on the understanding that laws and practices that respect the essence of the fundamental rights and freedoms and do not exceed what is necessary and proportionate in a democratic society to safeguard one of the objectives listed in Article 23(1) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679, are not in contradiction with these clauses.  (b)  The Parties declare that in providing the warranty in paragraph 14(a), they have taken due account in particular of the following elements:  (i)  the specific circumstances of the transfer, including the length of the processing chain, the number of actors involved and the transmission channels used; intended onward transfers; the type of recipient; the purpose of processing; the categories and format of the transferred personal data; the economic sector in which the transfer occurs; the storage location of the data transferred;   (ii)  the laws and practices of the third country of destination  including those requiring the disclosure of data to public authorities or authorising access by such authorities   relevant in light of the specific circumstances of the transfer, and the applicable limitations and safeguards ;  (iii)  any relevant contractual, technical or organisational safeguards put in place to supplement the safeguards under these clauses, including measures applied during transmission and to the processing of the personal data in the country of destination.  (c)  The Data Importer warrants that, in carrying out the assessment under paragraph 14(a), it has made its best efforts to provide the Data Exporter with relevant information and agrees that it will continue to cooperate with the Data Exporter in ensuring compliance with these clauses.  (d)  The Parties agree to document the assessment under paragraph 14(a) and make it available to the competent supervisory authority on request.  (e)  The Data Importer agrees to notify the Data Exporter promptly if, after having agreed to these clauses and for the duration of the contract, it has reason to believe that it is or has become subject to laws or practices not in line with the requirements under paragraph 14(a), including following a change in the laws of the third country or a measure (such as a disclosure request) indicating an application of such laws in practice that is not in line with the requirements in paragraph 14(a).   (f)  Following a notification pursuant to paragraph 14(a), or if the Data Exporter otherwise has reason to believe that the Data Importer can no longer fulfil its obligations under these clauses, the Data Exporter shall promptly identify appropriate measures (e.g. technical or organisational measures to ensure security and confidentiality) to be adopted by the Data Exporter and/or Data Importer to address the situation. The Data Exporter shall suspend the data transfer if it considers that no appropriate safeguards for such transfer can be ensured, or if instructed by the competent supervisory authority to do so. In this case, the Data Exporter shall be entitled to terminate the contract, insofar as it concerns the processing of personal data under these clauses. If the contract involves more than two Parties, the Data Exporter may exercise this right to termination only with respect to the relevant Party, unless the Parties have agreed otherwise. Where the contract is terminated pursuant to this clause, clause 16(d) and 16(e) shall apply.  15.  OBLIGATIONS OF THE DATA IMPORTER IN CASE OF ACCESS BY PUBLIC AUTHORITIES  15.1  Notification  (a)  The Data Importer agrees to notify the Data Exporter and, where possible, the data subject promptly (if necessary with the help of the Data Exporter) if it:  (i)  receives a legally binding request from a public authority, including judicial authorities, under the laws of the country of destination for the disclosure of personal data transferred pursuant to these clauses; such notification shall include information about the personal data requested, the requesting authority, the legal basis for the request and the response provided; or  (ii)  becomes aware of any direct access by public authorities to personal data transferred pursuant to these clauses in accordance with the laws of the country of destination; such notification shall include all information available to the importer.  (b)  If the Data Importer is prohibited from notifying the Data Exporter and/or the data subject under the laws of the country of destination, the Data Importer agrees to use its best efforts to obtain a waiver of the prohibition, with a view to communicating as much information as possible, as soon as possible. The Data Importer agrees to document its best efforts in order to be able to demonstrate them on request of the Data Exporter.  (c)  Where permissible under the laws of the country of destination, the Data Importer agrees to provide the Data Exporter, at regular intervals for the duration of the contract, with as much relevant information as possible on the requests received (in particular, number of requests, type of data requested, requesting authority/ies, whether requests have been challenged and the outcome of such challenges, etc.).   (d)  The Data Importer agrees to preserve the information pursuant to paragraphs 15.1(a) to (c) for the duration of the contract and make it available to the competent supervisory authority on request.  (e)  Paragraphs 15.1(a) to (c) are without prejudice to the obligation of the Data Importer pursuant to clause 14(e) and clause 16 to inform the Data Exporter promptly where it is unable to comply with these clauses.  15.2  Review of Legality and Data Minimisation  (a)  The Data Importer agrees to review the legality of the request for disclosure, in particular whether it remains within the powers granted to the requesting public authority, and to challenge the request if, after careful assessment, it concludes that there are reasonable grounds to consider that the request is unlawful under the laws of the country of destination, applicable obligations under international law and principles of international comity. The Data Importer shall, under the same conditions, pursue possibilities of appeal. When challenging a request, the Data Importer shall seek interim measures with a view to suspending the effects of the request until the competent judicial authority has decided on its merits. It shall not disclose the personal data requested until required to do so under the applicable procedural rules. These requirements are without prejudice to the obligations of the Data Importer under clause 14(e).  (b)  The Data Importer agrees to document its legal assessment and any challenge to the request for disclosure and, to the extent permissible under the laws of the country of destination, make the documentation available to the Data Exporter. It shall also make it available to the competent supervisory authority on request.   (c)  The Data Importer agrees to provide the minimum amount of information permissible when responding to a request for disclosure, based on a reasonable interpretation of the request.   SECTION 4 FINAL PROVISIONS  16.  NON COMPLIANCE WITH THE CLAUSES AND TERMINATION  (a)  The Data Importer shall promptly inform the Data Exporter if it is unable to comply with these clauses, for whatever reason.  (b)  In the event that the Data Importer is in breach of these clauses or unable to comply with these clauses, the Data Exporter shall suspend the transfer of personal data to the Data Importer until compliance is again ensured or the contract is terminated. This is without prejudice to clause 14(f).  (c)  The Data Exporter shall be entitled to terminate the contract, insofar as it concerns the processing of personal data under these clauses, where:  (i)  the Data Exporter has suspended the transfer of personal data to the Data Importer pursuant to paragraph 16(b) and compliance with these clauses is not restored within a reasonable time and in any event within one month of suspension;  (ii)  the Data Importer is in substantial or persistent breach of these clauses; or  (iii)  the Data Importer fails to comply with a binding decision of a competent court or supervisory authority regarding its obligations under these clauses.  In these cases, it shall inform the competent supervisory authority of such non  compliance. Where the contract involves more than two Parties, the Data Exporter may exercise this right to termination only with respect to the relevant Party, unless the Parties have agreed otherwise.  (d)  Personal data that has been transferred prior to the termination of the contract pursuant to paragraph 16(c) shall at the choice of the Data Exporter immediately be returned to the Data Exporter or deleted in its entirety. The same shall apply to any copies of the data. The Data Importer shall certify the deletion of the data to the Data Exporter. Until the data is deleted or returned, the Data Importer shall continue to ensure compliance with these clauses. In case of local laws applicable to the Data Importer that prohibit the return or deletion of the transferred personal data, the Data Importer warrants that it will continue to ensure compliance with these clauses and will only process the data to the extent and for as long as required under that local law.  (e)  Either Party may revoke its agreement to be bound by these clauses where (i) the European Commission adopts a decision pursuant to Article 45(3) of Regulation (EU) 2016/679 that covers the transfer of personal data to which these clauses apply; or (ii) Regulation (EU) 2016/679 becomes part of the legal framework of the country to which the personal data is transferred. This is without prejudice to other obligations applying to the processing in question under Regulation (EU) 2016/679.  17.  GOVERNING LAW  These clauses shall be governed by the law of one of the EU Member States, provided such law allows for third party beneficiary rights. The Parties agree that this shall be the law of Germany .  18.  CHOICE OF FORUM AND JURISDICTION  (a)  Any dispute arising from these clauses shall be resolved by the courts of an EU Member State.  (b)  The Parties agree that those shall be the courts of Germany.  (c)  A data subject may also bring legal proceedings against the Data Exporter and/or Data Importer before the courts of the Member State in which he/she has his/her habitual residence.  (d)  The Parties agree to submit themselves to the jurisdiction of such courts. clauses.description: 15. RESPONSIBLE ARTIFICIAL INTELLIGENCE from Regulatory Schedule  For the purposes of this paragraph 15, the following capitalised terms shall have the following meanings:  'AI' means Artificial Intelligence technologies that assist or replace human decision making,  including machine learning (ML) techniques, supervised and unsupervised learning.  'AI Driven Decisions' means decisions and decision outcomes made or supported by AI.  'AI Models' means specific AI algorithms and algorithmic processes utilised by the Supplier in the Supplies, which are used to arrive at AI Driven Decisions.  'RAI Principles' means the Responsible AI Principles set out in the Responsible AI Principles Annexure hereto.  15.1 The Supplier represents and warrants that in relation to any AI used in connection with the Supplies under the Contract Document:  15.1.1 has been fully disclosed to the Customer;  15.1.2 complies with the RAI Principles; and  15.1.3 produces sufficient information, logs, documentation, reports or other evidence to adequately explain all AI Driven Decisions and to demonstrate compliance with the RAI Principles including without limitation the information contemplated under paragraph 15.3.  15.2 The Supplier shall provide the Customer with no less than one hundred and twenty (120) days' notice prior to implementing any material change to AI Models or the use of AI in connection with the Supplies. The Supplier shall assist the Customer to understand the change and any potential impact on any AI Driven Decisions and demonstrate the Supplier's continued compliance with the RAI Principles.  15.3 Upon the Customer's written request, the Supplier shall provide any relevant information, logs, documentation, reports or other evidence requested by the Customer in relation to:  15.3.1 data inputs utilised and/or incorporated in the AI Models and detailed justifications relating to the need for such data inputs in each of the AI Models, together with an explanation of the outcomes produced through the use of each AI Model;  15.3.2 the Supplier's selection of a suitable training data set and how the Supplier made its assessment of training data for unjust bias, for adequate quality and that the data set covers a sufficient duration of time and is representative of the target population, to ensure a fair and representative data sample in any AI Models;  15.3.3 the basis on which the Supplier considers that each AI Model has been adequately trained using the selected data set;  15.3.4 details on how the input data has been and/or will be monitored for characteristics consistent with the data on which the AI Models were trained (e.g. change in distribution, data drift, bias creep);  15.3.5 details on how the AI Models have been and/or will be validated;  15.3.6 details on how the AI Models have been and/or will be assessed for unjust bias in the outcome (including evidence of related testing);  15.3.7 details on how the performance of the AI Models (covering stability, accuracy an unjust bias outcomes) have been and/or will be monitored on an ongoing basis; and  15.3.8 any outcomes or issues identified by Customer arising from the conduct of privacy and information and cyber security assessments under the Contract Document or in respect of the Supplier.  15.4 Without limiting paragraph 15.3 the Supplier shall provide such assistance and information as is reasonably necessary to enable the Customer and each Customer Group member to comply with any transparency requirements under Applicable Law, including customer facing statements or regulatory authority disclosures regarding the use of AI Models.    RESPONSIBLE AI PRINCIPLES ANNEXURE  1  Definitions  Unless the context otherwise requires, capitalised terms used but not defined in this Annexure shall have the meaning given to them in the Contract Document.  2  Responsible AI Principles Annexure  The AI solution shall be designed and be operated in line with each of the following principles:  2.1  Data Suitability: Ensure bias avoidance and adherence to Applicable Laws with respect to the types and sufficiency of data used during training, testing, and business as usual operation of the AI Model.  2.2  Explainability: Ensure adequate explanations are available for AI driven outcomes to prevent black box systems from being used and ensure justification for use of personal attributes.  2.3  Fairness/ Bias/ Ethics: Ensure decisions derived from the AI Models do not systematically disadvantage individuals or group of individuals (protected groups) as determined using assessment techniques and standards consistent with good financial industry practice.  2.4  Stability/ Overfitting: Ensure behaviour of AI model is consistent over the expected life of the model and avoid overfitting of the AI model to the training data.  2.5  Assessment Validation: Ensure sufficient independent oversight and validation of the AI Model.  2.6  Ongoing Monitoring: Ensure definition of metrics, thresholds, stable and accurate performance of AI Models to monitor after implementation, and suitable processes for monitoring and resolution of identified exceptions.  2.7  Auditability: Ensure algorithms, hyper parameters, test and training data, and related artefacts are preserved to support AI Model outcomes. Auditability also applies to business as usual where decisions and outcomes of the AI Model may have to be substantiated in case of a Customer or individual subject query, audit or regulatory responses.  2.8  Third Party Oversight: To the extent the Customer has granted consent to engage other third party (parties) in connection with the Supplies under the Contract Document, ensure adequate visibility of such externally sourced models to minimise the use of AI 'black box' solution, and appropriate due diligence of the other party (parties) engaged to develop AI solutions.

clauses.description: 2.  ANTI BRIBERY AND CORRUPTION  2.1  The Supplier represents, warrants and undertakes that it:  2.1.1  has not and shall not, and shall ensure to the fullest extent possible that its employees and agents and others under its direction or control do not, make or offer, directly or indirectly, any payment, gift or other advantage which: (i) would violate any applicable anti bribery or anti corruption laws, including but not limited to the United Kingdom Bribery Act 2010 and United States Foreign Corrupt Practices Act 1977; (ii) is intended to, or does, influence or reward any person for acting in breach of an expectation of good faith, impartiality or trust, or would otherwise be improper for the recipient to accept; (iii) is made to or for a Public Official with the intention of influencing them to obtain or retain an advantage in the conduct of business; or (iv) or would otherwise be unethical, illegal or improper;  2.1.2  is and shall remain compliant with all anti bribery and anti corruption laws and regulations: (i) in the country or countries in which Supplies are provided; and (ii) otherwise applicable to the Supplier;  2.1.3  shall not knowingly do or omit to do anything that may cause the Customer to be in breach of applicable anti bribery or anti corruption laws, including but not limited to the United Kingdom Bribery Act 2010 and United States Foreign Corrupt Practices Act 1977; and  2.1.4  shall, unless otherwise prohibited by applicable law, disclose to the Customer all details of any non compliance with paragraphs 2.1.1, 2.1.2 and 2.1.3 of this Schedule, and any non compliance by any other person envisaged by paragraph 2.2 immediately, upon becoming aware of such non compliance.  2.2  If the Supplier subcontracts to another person any right or obligation under the Contract Document or has another person perform any act or not perform any act in relation to the Contract Document, the Supplier shall procure such other person to comply with paragraph 2 of this Schedule or clauses materially equivalent, such that all forms of bribery and corruption are expressly prohibited, and the Supplier shall remain liable to the Customer for the performance by such person of such obligations as permissible under relevant law. For the purposes of this paragraph, a 'person' is any individual, partnership, company or any other legal entity, public or private.  2.3  It is a condition of the Contract Document that the Supplier complies with paragraphs 2.1 and 2.2 of this Schedule. If the Supplier breaches, or the Customer reasonably believes that the Supplier has breached, paragraph 2.1 or paragraph 2.2, without prejudice to any other remedy available to the Customer, the Customer may terminate the Contract Document upon written notice to the Supplier, and the Supplier shall not be entitled to receive any compensation in respect of such termination.  2.4  For the purpose of this paragraph 2, the following capitalised terms shall have the following meanings:  Politically Exposed Persons means: (i) an individual who is or has, at any time, been entrusted with prominent public functions; or (ii) an immediate family member, or known close associate of the individual entrusted with a prominent public function.    Public Official means:  (i)  any officer, employee or representative of a government, at any level of seniority, whether national, federal or local (for example, regulators, legislators, customs officials, central bank governors, ministers, and tribal authorities);    (ii)  any individual exercising a legislative, administrative, regulatory, judicial or other public or official function, whether appointed or elected;    (iii)  any officer, employee or representative, at any level, of a State Owned Entity;   (iv)  members of royal families;    (v)  representatives of political parties and political candidates;    (vi)  any officer, employee or representative of a public international organisation (for example the EU, World Bank, IMF and UN);  (vii)  private individuals or entities contracted by a State Owned Entity or the government to exercise a public function;    (viii)  Politically Exposed Persons; and   (ix)  any other parties determined to be a 'sovereign', 'public official', 'government official', or the like, under local law.  State Owned Entity means any government or state owned or controlled entity, instrumentality or department where the government or state:   (i)  has 50% or more ownership,    (ii)  board or voting control, or   (iii)  is otherwise deemed to have a golden share, or the power to veto key shareholder decisions.  Examples include, but are not limited to: sovereign wealth funds, state controlled utilities and hospitals, pension funds, and central banks.  An entity does not cease to be a State Owned Entity if it purports to act in a private capacity (e.g. a public company traded on a securities exchange) or by the fact that services are rendered without compensation.
